title: Glossary
heading: APPENDIX A EM 1110-2-1100 GLOSSARY OF COASTAL TERMINOLOGY (Change 1)
text: 31 July 2003

title: Glossary
heading: ABRASION
text: The mechanical wearing away by rock material transported by wind or water

title: Glossary
heading: ABRASION PLATFORM
text: A rock or clay platform which has been worn by the processes of abrasion.

title: Glossary
heading: ACCELEROMETER
text: A device used in wave buoys for measuring acceleration.

title: Glossary
heading: ACCRETION
text: May be either natural or artificial. Natural accretion is the buildup of land, solely by the action of the forces of nature, on a beach by deposition of water- or airborne material. Artificial accretion is a similar buildup of land by reason of an act of man, such as the accretion formed by a GROIN, BREAKWATER, or beach fill deposited by mechanical means. Also AGGRADATION.

title: Glossary
heading: ACTIVE MARGIN
text: A margin of a continental plate consisting of a continental shelf and slope, and an oceanic trench or basin.

title: Glossary
heading: ADJUSTABLE GROIN
text: A GROIN whose permeability can be changed, usually with gates or removable sections.

title: Glossary
heading: ADVANCE (of a beach)
text: (1) A continuing seaward movement of the shoreline. (2) A net seaward movement of the shoreline over a specified time. Also PROGRESSION.

title: Glossary
heading: ADVECTION
text: Changes in a sea water property (salinity, temperature, oxygen content, etc.) that take place in the presence of currents. Also, changes in atmospheric properties in the earth's atmosphere.

title: Glossary
heading: AEOLIAN
text: See EOLIAN.

title: Glossary
heading: AGE, WAVE
text: The ratio of wave velocity to wind velocity (in wave forecasting theory).

title: Glossary
heading: AGGRADATION
text: See ACCRETION.

title: Glossary
heading: ALIGNMENT
text: The course along which the center line of a channel, canal or drain is located.

title: Glossary
heading: ALLUVIAL DEPOSITS
text: Detrital material which is transported by a river and deposited – usually temporarily – at points along the flood plain of a river. Commonly composed of sands and gravels.

title: Glossary
heading: ALLUVIAL PLANE
text: A plain bordering a river, formed by the deposition of material eroded from areas of higher elevation.

title: Glossary
heading: ALLUVIUM
text: Soil (sand, mud, or similar detrital material) deposited by streams, or the deposits formed.

title: Glossary
heading: ALONGSHORE
text: Parallel to and near the shoreline; LONGSHORE.

title: Glossary
heading: ALTIMETER
text: An instrument that determines its distance above a particular surface.

title: Glossary
heading: ALTIMETER, LASER
text: An instrument that determines altitude by measuring the length of time needed for a pulse of coherent light to travel from the instrument to the surface and back, and multiplies half this time by the speed of light to get the straight-line distance to the surface.

title: Glossary
heading: ALTIMETER, LIDAR
text: See ALTIMETER, LASER.

title: Glossary
heading: AMPLITUDE, WAVE
text: (1) The magnitude of the displacement of a wave from a mean value. An ocean wave has an amplitude equal to the vertical distance from still-water level to wave crest. For a sinusoidal wave, the amplitude is one-half the wave height. (2) The semirange of a constituent tide.

title: Glossary
heading: ANCHOR ICE
text: Spongy underwater ice formed on a submerged object or attached to the bottom of a shallow body of water which is itself not frozen; syn. bottom ice

title: Glossary
heading: ANGLE OF REPOSE
text: The maximum slope (measured from the horizontal) at which soils and loose materials on the banks of canals, rivers or embankments will stay stable.

title: Glossary
heading: ANISOTROPIC
text: Having properties that change with changing directions.

title: Glossary
heading: ANOXIC
text: Refers to an environment that contain little or no dissolved oxygen and hence little or no benthic marine life. These conditions arise in some basins or fjords where physical circulation of seawater is limited.

title: Glossary
heading: ANTIDUNES
text: BED FORMS that occur in trains and are in phase with, and strongly interact with, gravity water-surface waves.

title: Glossary
heading: ANTINODE
text: See LOOP.

title: Glossary
heading: APRON
text: Layer of stone, concrete or other material to protect the toe of a structure.

title: Glossary
heading: AQUATIC MATERIAL PLACEMENT
text: Comprises all placement options under which the dredged material is submerged under water and remains water-saturated after placement terminates.

title: Glossary
heading: AQUIFER
text: A geologic formation that is water-bearing, and which transmits water from one point in the formation to another.

title: Glossary
heading: ARCHIPELAGO
text: A sea that contains numerous islands; also the island group itself.

title: Glossary
heading: ARMOR LAYER
text: Protective layer on a BREAKWATER or SEAWALL composed of armor units.

title: Glossary
heading: ARMOR UNIT or STONE
text: A relatively large quarrystone or concrete shape that is selected to fit specified geometric characteristics and density. It is usually of nearly uniform size and usually large enough to require individual placement. In normal cases it is used as primary wave protection and is placed in thicknesses of at least two units.

title: Glossary
heading: ARTIFICIAL NOURISHMENT
text: The process of replenishing a beach with material (usually sand) obtained from another location.

title: Glossary
heading: ASTRONOMICAL TIDE
text: The tidal levels and character which would result from gravitational effects, e.g. of the Earth, Sun and Moon, without any atmospheric influences.

title: Glossary
heading: ATOLL
text: A ring-shaped coral REEF, often carrying low sand islands, enclosing a shallow LAGOON. The reef is surrounded by deep water of the open sea.

title: Glossary
heading: ATTENUATION
text: (1) A lessening of the amplitude of a wave with distance from the origin. (2) The decrease of water-particle motion with increasing depth. Particle motion resulting from surface oscillatory waves attenuates rapidly with depth, and practically disappears at a depth equal to a surface wavelength.

title: Glossary
heading: AUTOCHTHONOUS
text: A term applied to shelves on which older shelf sediments are primarily being reworked by modern shelf processes.

title: Glossary
heading: AUTOMATIC TIDE GAGE
text: An instrument that automatically registers the rise and fall of the tide. In some instruments, the registration is accomplished by printing the heights at regular intervals, in others by a continuous graph in which the height of the tide is represented by the ordinates of the curve and the corresponding time by the abscissae.

title: Glossary
heading: AVULSION
text: (1) Rapid EROSION of the shore land by waves during a storm. (2) A sudden cutting off of land by flood, currents or change in course of a body of water.

title: Glossary
heading: AWASH
text: Situated so that the top is intermittently washed by waves or tidal action. Condition of being exposed or just bare at any stage of the tide between high water and chart datum. B

title: Glossary
heading: BACK BARRIER
text: Pertaining to the lagoon-marsh-tidal creek complex in the lee of a coastal barrier island, barrier spit, or baymouth barrier.

title: Glossary
heading: BACKBEACH
text: See BACKSHORE.

title: Glossary
heading: BACKRUSH
text: The seaward return of the water following the uprush of the waves. For any given tide stage the point of farthest return seaward of the backrush is known as the Limit of backrush or limit backwash.

title: Glossary
heading: BACKSHORE
text: That zone of the shore or beach lying between the foreshore and the coastline comprising the BERM or BERMS and acted upon by waves only during severe storms, especially when combined with exceptionally high water. Also BACKBEACH. (See Figure A-1)

title: Glossary
heading: BACKWASH
text: (1) See BACKRUSH. (2) Water or waves thrown back by an obstruction such as a ship, BREAKWATER, or cliff.

title: Glossary
heading: BACKWASH RIPPLES
text: Low amplitude ripple marks formed on fine sand beaches by the Backwash of the waves.

title: Glossary
heading: BACKWATER CURVE
text: The longitudinal profile of the water surface in an open channel where the depth of flow has been increased by an obstruction as a weir or a dam across the channel, by increase in channel roughness, by decrease in channel width or by a decrease of the bed gradient

title: Glossary
heading: BANK
text: (1) The rising ground bordering a lake, river, or sea; or of a river or channel, for which it is designated as right or left as the observer is facing downstream. (2) An elevation of the sea floor or large area, located on a continental (or island) shelf and over which the depth is relatively shallow but sufficient for safe surface navigation (e.g., Georges Bank); a group of shoals. (3) In its secondary sense, used only with a qualifying word such as "sandbank," "gravelbank," or "spoil bank," a shallow area consisting of shifting forms of silt, sand, mud, and gravel.

title: Glossary
heading: BAR
text: A submerged or emerged embankment of sand, gravel, or other unconsolidated material built on the sea floor in shallow water by waves and currents. See BAYMOUTH BAR, CUSPATE BAR.

title: Glossary
heading: BARRIER BEACH
text: A bar essentially parallel to the shore, the crest of which is above normal high water level. Also called offshore barrier and BARRIER ISLAND.

title: Glossary
heading: BARRIER FLAT
text: The flat area, often marshy and populated with low vegetation, on the bay or lagoon side of a barrier island Figure A-1. Definition of terms and features describing the coastal zone

title: Glossary
heading: BARRIER ISLAND
text: A detached portion of a barrier beach between two inlets. It commonly has DUNES, vegetated areas, and swampy terranes (see BARRIER FLAT) extending from the beach into the lagoon. Example: Outer Banks, North Carolina.

title: Glossary
heading: BARRIER LAGOON
text: A bay roughly parallel to the coast and separated from the open ocean by barrier islands. Also, the body of water encircled by coral islands and REEFS, in which case it may be called an ATOLL lagoon.

title: Glossary
heading: BARRIER REEF
text: A coral REEF parallel to and separated from the coast by a lagoon that is too deep for coral growth. Generally, barrier reefs follow the coasts for long distances and are cut through at irregular intervals by channels or passes. Example: Great Barrier Reef, Queensland, Australia.

title: Glossary
heading: BARRIER SPIT
text: Similar to a BARRIER ISLAND, but connected to the mainland.

title: Glossary
heading: BASIN
text: A depressed area with no surface outlet, such as a lake basin or an enclosed sea.

title: Glossary
heading: BASIN, BOAT
text: A naturally or artificially enclosed or nearly enclosed harbor area for small craft.

title: Glossary
heading: BASTION
text: A massive groin, or projecting section of seawall normally constructed with its crest above water level.

title: Glossary
heading: BATHYMETRIC CHART
text: A topographic map of the bed of the ocean, with depths indicated by contours (isobaths) drawn at regular intervals.

title: Glossary
heading: BATHYMETRY
text: The measurement of water depths in oceans, seas, and lakes; also information derived from such measurements.

title: Glossary
heading: BAY
text: A recess in the shore or an inlet of a sea between two capes or headlands, not as large as a gulf but larger than a cove. See also BIGHT, EMBAYMENT.

title: Glossary
heading: BAYMOUTH BAR
text: A bar extending partly or entirely across the mouth of a bay .

title: Glossary
heading: BAYOU
text: A minor sluggish waterway or estuarial creek, tributary to, or connecting, other streams or bodies of water, whose course is usually through lowlands or swamps. Sometimes called SLOUGH. Term is commonly used in the southern United States.

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH
text: The zone of unconsolidated material that extends landward from the low water line to the place where there is marked change in material or physiographic form, or to the line of permanent vegetation (usually the effective limit of storm waves). The seaward limit of a beach--unless otherwise specified--is the mean low water line. A beach includes foreshore and backshore. (See Figure A-1) See also SHORE, SUSTAINABLE BEACH, and SELF-SUSTAINING BEACH, and TIDELANDS.

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH ACCRETION
text: See ACCRETION.

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH BERM
text: A nearly horizontal part of the beach or backshore formed by the deposit of material by wave action. Some beaches have no berms, others have one or several. (See Figure A-1)

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH CREST
text: The point representing the limit of normal HIGH TIDE wave run-up (see BERM CREST)

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH CUSP
text: See CUSP.

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH EROSION
text: The carrying away of beach materials by wave action, tidal currents, littoral currents, or wind.

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH FACE
text: The section of the beach normally exposed to the action of the wave uprush. The FORESHORE of a BEACH. (Not synonymous with SHOREFACE.)

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH FILL
text: Material placed on a beach to renourish eroding shores, usually pumped by dredge but sometimes delivered by trucks.

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH HEAD
text: The cliff, dune or sea wall looming above the land ward limit of the active beach

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH MATERIAL
text: Granular sediments, usually sand or shingle moved by the sea.

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH NOURISHMENT
text: See BEACH FILL.

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH PLAN SHAPE
text: The shape of the beach in plan; usually shown as a contour line, combination of contour lines or recognizable features such as beach crest and/or the still water line

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH PROFILE
text: A cross-section taken perpendicular to a given beach contour; the profile may include the face of a dune or sea wall, extend over the backshore, across the foreshore, and seaward underwater into the NEARSHORE zone.

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH RIDGE
text: See RIDGE, BEACH.

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH SCARP
text: See SCARP, BEACH.

title: Glossary
heading: BEACH WIDTH
text: The horizontal dimension of the beach measured normal to the shoreline and landward of the higher-high tide line (on oceanic coasts) or from the still water level (on lake coasts)

title: Glossary
heading: BEAUFORT SCALE
text: Classification of the force of the winds in accordance with a scale established by Rear-Admiral, Sir Francis Beaufort in which the range of intensity varies in integers from 0 to 12, with a description of the state and behavior of a "wellconditioned man-of-war."

title: Glossary
heading: BED
text: The bottom of a watercourse, or any body of water.

title: Glossary
heading: BED FORMS
text: Any deviation from a flat bed that is readily detectable by eye and higher than the largest sediment size present in the parent bed material; generated on the bed of an alluvial channel by the flow.

title: Glossary
heading: BED LOAD
text: Sediment transport mode in which individual particles either roll or slide along the bed as a shallow, mobile layer a few particle diameters deep, the part of the load that is not continuously in suspension.

title: Glossary
heading: BED PROTECTION
text: A (rock) structure on the bed in order to protect the underlying bed against erosion due to current and/or wave action.

title: Glossary
heading: BED SHEAR STRESS
text: The way in which waves (or currents) transfer energy to the sea bed.

title: Glossary
heading: BEDDING PLANE
text: A surface parallel to the surface of deposition, which may or may not have a physical expression. The original attitude of a bedding plane should not be assumed to have been horizontal.

title: Glossary
heading: BEDROCK
text: The solid rock that underlies gravel, soil, and other superficial material. Bedrock may be exposed at the surface (an outcrop) or it may br buried under a few centimeters to thousands of meters of unconsolidated material.

title: Glossary
heading: BENCH
text: (1) A level or gently sloping erosion plane inclined seaward. (2) A nearly horizontal area at about the level of maximum high water on the sea side of a dike.

title: Glossary
heading: BENCH MARK, TIDAL
text: A bench mark whose elevation has been determined with respect to MEAN SEA LEVEL at a nearby tide GAUGE; the tidal bench mark is used as reference for that tide gauge.

title: Glossary
heading: BENCH MARK
text: A permanently fixed point of known elevation. A primary bench mark is one close to a tide station to which the tide staff and tidal datum originally are referenced.

title: Glossary
heading: BENEFICIAL USE OF DREDGED MATERIAL
text: Placement or use of dredged material for some productive purpose. Examples: BEACH FILL or NEARSHORE BERM construction.

title: Glossary
heading: BENEFITS
text: The asset value of a scheme, usually measured in terms of the cost of damages avoided by the scheme, or the valuation of perceived amenity or environmental improvements

title: Glossary
heading: BENTHIC
text: Pertaining to the sub-aquatic bottom.

title: Glossary
heading: BENTHOS
text: Those animals who live on the sediments of the sea floor, including both mobile and non-mobile forms.

title: Glossary
heading: BERM
text: (1) On a beach: a nearly horizontal plateau on the beach face or backshore, formed by the deposition of beach material by wave action or by means of a mechanical plant as part of a beach renourishment scheme. Some natural beaches have no berm, others have several. (2) On a structure: a nearly horizontal area, often built to support or key-in an armor layer.

title: Glossary
heading: BERM, BEACH
text: See BEACH BERM.

title: Glossary
heading: BERM BREAKWATER
text: Rubble mound structure with horizontal berm of armor stones at about sea level, which is allowed to be (re)shaped by the waves.

title: Glossary
heading: BERM CREST
text: The seaward limit of a BERM. Also called BERM EDGE. (See Figure A-1)

title: Glossary
heading: BIGHT
text: A bend in a coastline forming an open BAY. A BAY formed by such a bend.

title: Glossary
heading: BIOTURBATION
text: The disturbance of sediment bedding by the activities of burrowing organisms.

title: Glossary
heading: BIRDFOOT DELTA
text: A river DELTA formed by many levee-bordered DISTRIBUTARIES extending seaward and resembling in plan the outstretched claws of a bird. Example: Mississippi River delta.

title: Glossary
heading: BLANKET (FOUNDATION or BEDDING)
text: A layer or layers of graded fine stones underlying a BREAKWATER, GROIN or rock embankment to prevent the natural bed material from being washed away.

title: Glossary
heading: BLOWN SANDS
text: See EOLIAN SANDS.

title: Glossary
heading: BLOWOUT
text: A depression on the land surface caused by wind erosion.

title: Glossary
heading: BLUFF
text: A high, steep bank or cliff.

title: Glossary
heading: BOG
text: A wet, spongy, poorly drained area which is usually rich in very specialized plants, contains a high percentage of organic remnants and residues and frequently is associated with a spring, seepage area, or other subsurface water source. A bog sometimes represents the final stage of the natural processes of eutrophication by which lakes and other bodies of water are very slowly transformed into land areas.

title: Glossary
heading: BOIL
text: An upward flow of water in a sandy formation due to an unbalanced hydrostatic pressure resulting from a rise in a nearby stream, or from removing the overburden in making excavations.

title: Glossary
heading: BOLD COAST
text: A prominent landmass that rises steeply from the sea.

title: Glossary
heading: BORE
text: A very rapid rise of the tide in which the advancing water presents an abrupt front of considerable height. In shallow estuaries where the range of tide is large, the high water is propagated inward faster than the low water because of the greater depth at high water. If the high water overtakes the low water, an abrupt front is presented, with the high-water crest finally falling forward as the tide continues to advance. Also EAGER.

title: Glossary
heading: BOTTOM (nature of)
text: The composition or character of the bed of an ocean or other body of water (e.g., clay, coral, gravel, mud, ooze, pebbles, rock, shell, shingle, hard, or soft). (See Figure A-1)

title: Glossary
heading: BOTTOM BOUNDARY LAYER
text: The lower portion of the water flow that experiences frictional retardation based on its proximity to the bed.

title: Glossary
heading: BOTTOMSET
text: One of the horizontal or gently inclined sediment layers deposited in front of the advancing forest beds of a delta.

title: Glossary
heading: BOULDER
text: A rounded rock more than 256 mm (10 inch) in diameter; larger than a cobblestone. See SOIL CLASSIFICATION.

title: Glossary
heading: BOUNDARY CONDITIONS
text: Environmental conditions, e.g. waves, currents, drifts, etc. used as boundary input to physical or numerical models

title: Glossary
heading: BOX GAGE
text: A tide GAUGE that is operated by a float in a long vertical box to which the tide is admitted through an opening in the bottom. In the original type of box gauge the float supported a graduated rod which rose and fell with the tide.

title: Glossary
heading: BRAIDED RIVER
text: A river type with multiple channels separated by shoals, bars and islands

title: Glossary
heading: BREACHING
text: (1) Formation of a channel through a barrier spit or island by storm waves, tidal action, or river flow. Usually occurs after a greater than normal flow, such as during a hurricane. (2) Failure of a dike allowing flooding.

title: Glossary
heading: BREAKER
text: A wave breaking on a shore, over a REEF, etc. Breakers may be classified into four types (See Figure II-4-1): COLLAPSING--breaking occurs over lower half of wave, with minimal air pocket and usually no splash-up. Bubbles and foam present. PLUNGING--crest curls over air pocket; breaking is usually with a crash. Smooth splash-up usually follows. SPILLING--bubbles and turbulent water spill down front face of wave. The upper 25 percent of the front face may become vertical before breaking. Breaking generally occurs over quite a distance. SURGING--wave peaks up, but bottom rushes forward from under wave, and wave slides up beach face with little or no bubble production. Water surface remains almost plane except where ripples may be produced on the beachface during runback.

title: Glossary
heading: BREAKER DEPTH
text: The still-water depth at the point where a wave breaks. Also called BREAKING DEPTH.

title: Glossary
heading: BREAKER INDEX
text: Ratio of breaking wave height to DEEPWATER wave height

title: Glossary
heading: BREAKER ZONE
text: The zone within which waves approaching the coastline commence breaking, typically in water depths of between 5 and 10 meters for ocean coasts, but sometimes in shallower water.

title: Glossary
heading: BREAKING
text: Reduction in wave energy and height in the surf zone due to limited water depth

title: Glossary
heading: BREAKWATER
text: A man-made structure protecting a shore area, harbor, anchorage, or basin from waves. A harbor work.

title: Glossary
heading: BREASTWORK
text: Vertically-faced or steeply inclined structure usually built with timber and parallel to the shoreline, at or near the beach crest, to resist erosion or mitigate against flooding.

title: Glossary
heading: BUFFER AREA
text: A parcel or strip of land that is designed and designated to permanently remain vegetated in an undisturbed and natural condition to protect an adjacent aquatic or wetland site from upland impacts, to provide habitat for wildlife and to afford limited public access.

title: Glossary
heading: BULKHEAD
text: A structure or partition to retain or prevent sliding of the land. A secondary purpose is to protect the UPLAND against damage from wave action.

title: Glossary
heading: BULK DENSITY
text: Identical to apparent specific gravity, Ga = soil density ÷ density of water. "Apparent" means that the basis of measurement is the total soil volume including voids, not just the individual soil particles.

title: Glossary
heading: BULL NOSE
text: Substantial lip or protuberance at the top of the seaward face of a wall, to deflect waves seaward.

title: Glossary
heading: BUOY
text: A float; especially a floating object moored to the bottom, to mark a channel, anchor, shoal rock, etc. Some common types include: a nun or nut buoy is conical in shape; a can buoy is squat and cylindrical above water and conical below water; a spar buoy is a vertical, slender spar anchored at one end; a bell buoy, bearing a bell, runs mechanically or by the action of waves, usually marks shoals or rocks; a whistling buoy, similarly operated, marks shoals or channel entrances; a dan buoy carries a pole with a flag or light on it.

title: Glossary
heading: BUOYANCY
text: The resultant of upward forces, exerted by the water on a submerged or floating body, equal to the weight of the water displaced by this body.

title: Glossary
heading: BYPASSING, SAND
text: Hydraulic or mechanical movement of sand from the accreting updrift side to the eroding downdrift side of an inlet or harbor entrance. The hydraulic movement may include natural movement as well as movement caused by man. C

title: Glossary
heading: CAISSON
text: Concrete box-type structure.

title: Glossary
heading: CALCAREOUS
text: Containing calcium carbonate $(CaCO {3}),$ chiefly as the minerals calcite and aragonite. When applied to rock, it implies that as much as 50 percent of the rock is carbonate (e.g., calcareous sand).

title: Glossary
heading: CALM
text: The condition of the water surface when there is no WIND WAVES or SWELL.

title: Glossary
heading: CANAL
text: An artificial watercourse cut through a land area for such uses as navigation and irrigation.

title: Glossary
heading: CANYON
text: A relatively narrow, deep depression with steep slopes, the bottom of which grades continuously downward. May be underwater (submarine) or on land (SUBAERIAL).

title: Glossary
heading: CAPE
text: A land area jutting seaward from a continent or large island which prominently marks a change in, or interrupts notably, the coastal trend; a prominent feature. Examples: Cape Cod, Massachusetts; Cape Hatteras, North Carolina.

title: Glossary
heading: CAPILLARY WAVE
text: A wave whose velocity of propagation is controlled primarily by the surface tension of the liquid in which the wave is traveling. Water waves of length less than about 1 inch are considered capillary waves. Waves longer than 1 inch and shorter than 2 inches are in an indeterminate zone between capillary and gravity waves. See RIPPLE.

title: Glossary
heading: CARTOGRAPHY
text: The science and art of making maps.

title: Glossary
heading: CATCHMENT AREA
text: The area which drains naturally to a particular point on a river, thus contributing to its natural discharge.

title: Glossary
heading: CAUSEWAY
text: A raised road across wet or marshy ground, or across water.

title: Glossary
heading: CAUSTIC
text: In refraction of waves, the name given to the curve to which adjacent orthogonals of waves refracted by a bottom whose contour lines are curved, are tangents. The occurrence of a caustic always marks a region of crossed orthogonals and high wave convergence.

title: Glossary
heading: CAY
text: See KEY.

title: Glossary
heading: CELERITY
text: Wave speed.

title: Glossary
heading: CENTRAL PRESSURE INDEX (CPI)
text: The estimated minimum barometric pressure in the eye (approximate center) of a particular hurricane. The CPI is considered the most stable index to intensity of hurricane wind velocities in the periphery of the storm; the highest wind speeds are associated with storms having the lowest CPI.

title: Glossary
heading: CHANNEL
text: (1) A natural or artificial waterway of perceptible extent which either periodically or continuously contains moving water, or which forms a connecting link between two bodies of water. (2) The part of a body of water deep enough to be used for navigation through an area otherwise too shallow for navigation. (3) A large strait, as the English Channel. (4) The deepest part of a stream, bay, or strait through which the main volume or current of water flows (see THALWEG).

title: Glossary
heading: CHANNEL CAPACITY
text: The maximum flow which a channel is capable of transmitting without its banks being overtopped.

title: Glossary
heading: CHANNEL-MOUTH BAR
text: A bar built where a stream enters a body of standing water, resulting from decreased flow velocity.

title: Glossary
heading: CHARACTERISTIC WAVE HEIGHT
text: See SIGNIFICANT WAVE HEIGHT.

title: Glossary
heading: CHART
text: A special-purpose map, esp. one designed for navigation such as a bathymetric chart .

title: Glossary
heading: CHART DATUM
text: The plane or level to which soundings (or elevations) or tide heights are referenced (usually LOW WATER DATUM). The surface is called a tidal datum when referred to a certain phase of tide. To provide a safety factor for navigation, some level lower than MEAN SEA LEVEL is generally selected for hydrographic charts, such as MEAN LOW WATER or MEAN LOWER LOW WATER. See DATUM PLANE.

title: Glossary
heading: CHEMICAL WEATHERING
text: Disintegration of rocks and sediments by chemical alteration of the constituent minerals or of the cementing matrix. It is caused by exposure, oxidation, temperature changes, and biological processes.

title: Glossary
heading: CHENIER
text: A long, narrow wooded beach ridge or sandy hummock forming roughly parallel to a prograding shore, usually seaward of marsh and mud-flat deposits (as along the south coast of Louisiana)

title: Glossary
heading: CHOP
text: The short-crested waves that may spring up quickly in a moderate breeze, and which break easily at the crest. Also WIND CHOP.

title: Glossary
heading: CHOPPY SEA
text: Short, rough waves tumbling with a short and quick motion. Short-crested waves that may spring up quickly in a moderate breeze, and break easily at the crest.

title: Glossary
heading: CLAPOTIS
text: The French equivalent for a type of STANDING WAVE. In American usage it is usually associated with the standing wave phenomenon caused by the reflection of a nonbreaking wave train from a structure with a face that is vertical or nearly vertical. Full clapotis is one with 100 percent reflection of the incident wave; partial clapotis is one with less than 100 percent reflection.

title: Glossary
heading: CLASTIC ROCKS
text: Rocks built up of fragments which have been produced by weathering and erosion of pre-existing rocks and minerals and, typically, transported mechanically to their point of deposition.

title: Glossary
heading: CLAY
text: A fine grained, plastic, sediment with a typical grain size less than 0.004 mm. Possesses electromagnetic properties which bind the grains together to give a bulk strength or cohesion. See SOIL CLASSIFICATION.

title: Glossary
heading: CLIFF
text: A high, steep face of rock; a precipice. See also SEA CLIFF.

title: Glossary
heading: CLIMATE
text: The characteristic weather of a region, particularly regarding temperature and precipitation, averaged over some significant internal of time (years).

title: Glossary
heading: CLOSING LINE
text: The line dividing inland waters and the territorial sea at the mouth of a river, bay, or harbor.

title: Glossary
heading: CLOSURE DEPTH
text: The water depth beyond which repetitive profile or topographic surveys (collected over several years) do not detect vertical sea bed changes, generally considered the seaward limit of littoral transport. The depth can be determined from repeated cross-shore profile surveys or estimated using formulas based on wave statistics. Note that this does not imply the lack of sediment motion beyond this depth.

title: Glossary
heading: CNOIDAL WAVE
text: A type of wave in shallow water (i.e., where the depth of water is less than 1/8 to 1/10 the wavelength). The surface profile is expressed in terms of the Jacobian elliptic function cn u; hence the term cnoidal.

title: Glossary
heading: CO-TIDAL LINES
text: Lines which link all the points where TIDE is at the same stage (or phase) of its cycle.

title: Glossary
heading: COAST
text: (1) A strip of land of indefinite width (may be several kilometers) that extends from the SHORELINE inland to the first major change in terrain features. (See Figure A-1.) (2) The part of a country regarded as near the coast.

title: Glossary
heading: COASTAL AREA
text: The land and sea area bordering the SHORELINE. (See Figure A-1)

title: Glossary
heading: COASTAL CURRENTS
text: (1) Those currents which flow roughly parallel to the shore and constitute a relatively uniform drift in the deeper water adjacent to the surf zone. These currents may be tidal currents, transient, wind-driven currents, or currents associated with the distribution of mass in local waters. (2) For navigational purposes, the term is used to designate a current in coastwise shipping lanes where the tidal current is frequently rotary.

title: Glossary
heading: COASTAL DEFENSE
text: General term used to encompass both coast protection against erosion and sea defense against flooding.

title: Glossary
heading: COASTAL FORCING
text: The natural processes which drive coastal hydro- and morphodynamics (e.g.winds, waves, tides, etc).

title: Glossary
heading: COASTAL PLAIN
text: The plain composed of horizontal or gently sloping strata of clastic materials, generally representing a strip of sea bottom that has emerged from the sea in recent geologic time. May extend inland many km.

title: Glossary
heading: COASTAL PROCESSES
text: Collective term covering the action of natural forces on the SHORELINE, and near shore seabed

title: Glossary
heading: COASTAL STRIP
text: A zone directly adjacent to the waterline, where only coast related activities take place. Usually this is a strip of some 100 m wide. In this strip the coastal defense activities take place. In this strip often there are restrictions to land use.

title: Glossary
heading: COASTAL ZONE
text: The transition zone where the land meets water, the region that is directly influenced by marine and lacustrine hydrodynamic processes. Extends offshore to the continental shelf break and onshore to the first major change in topography above the reach of major storm waves. On barrier coasts, includes the bays and LAGOONS between the BARRIER and the mainland.

title: Glossary
heading: COASTAL ZONE MANAGEMENT
text: The integrated and general development of the coastal zone. Coastal Zone Management is not restricted to coastal defense works, but includes also a development in economical, ecological and social terms. Coastline Management is a part of Coastal Zone Management.

title: Glossary
heading: COAST LINE
text: The term used in the Submerged Lands Act to describe the low-water line and closing lines across the mouths of inland water bodies.

title: Glossary
heading: COASTLINE
text: (1) Technically, the line that forms the boundary between the coast and the shore. (2) Commonly, the line that forms the boundary between the land and the water, esp. the water of a sea or ocean. The SHORELINE. A more general term than COAST LINE.

title: Glossary
heading: COBBLE (COBBLESTONE)
text: A rock fragment between 64 and 256 mm in diameter, usually rounded. See SOIL CLASSIFICATION.

title: Glossary
heading: COFFERDAM
text: A temporary watertight structure enclosing all or part of the construction area so that construction can proceed in the dry.

title: Glossary
heading: COHESIVE SEDIMENT
text: Sediment containing significant proportion of clays, the electromagnetic properties of which cause the sediment to bind together.

title: Glossary
heading: COLLOID
text: As a size term refers to particles smaller than 0.00024 mm, smaller than clay size.

title: Glossary
heading: COMBER
text: (1) A DEEPWATER wave whose crest is pushed forward by a strong wind; much larger than a whitecap. (2) A long-period breaker.

title: Glossary
heading: COMPETENCE
text: The ability of a wind or water current to transport detritus, in terms of particle size rather than amount, measured as the diameter of the largest particles.

title: Glossary
heading: COMPLEX SPIT
text: A large RECURVED SPIT with secondary spits developed at its end. Example: Sandy Hook, New Jersey.

title: Glossary
heading: CONFLUENCE
text: The junction of two or more river reaches or channels (the opposite of a bifurcation) .

title: Glossary
heading: CONSOLIDATION
text: The gradual, slow compression of a cohesive soil due to weight acting on it, which occurs as water is driven out of the voids in the soil. Consolidation only occurs in clays or other soils of low permeability.

title: Glossary
heading: CONTINENTAL SHELF
text: (1) The zone bordering a continent extending from the line of permanent immersion to the depth, usually about 100 m to 200 m, where there is a marked or rather steep descent toward the great depths of the ocean. (2) The area under active littoral processes during the HOLOCENE period. (3) The region of the oceanic bottom that extends outward from the shoreline with an average slope of less than 1:100, to a line where the gradient begins to exceed 1:40 (the CONTINENTAL SLOPE).

title: Glossary
heading: CONTINENTAL SLOPE
text: The declivity from the offshore border of the CONTINENTAL SHELF to oceanic depths. It is characterized by a marked increase in slope.

title: Glossary
heading: CONTOUR
text: A line on a map or chart representing points of equal elevation with relation to a DATUM. It is called an ISOBATH when connecting points of equal depth below a datum. Also called DEPTH CONTOUR.

title: Glossary
heading: CONTROLLING DEPTH
text: The least depth in the navigable parts of a waterway, governing the maximum draft of vessels that can enter.

title: Glossary
heading: CONVERGENCE
text: (1) In refraction phenomena, the decreasing of the distance between orthogonals in the direction of wave travel. Denotes an area of increasing wave height and energy concentration. (2) In wind-setup phenomena, the increase in setup observed over that which would occur in an equivalent rectangular basin of uniform depth, caused by changes in planform or depth; also the decrease in basin width or depth causing such increase in setup.

title: Glossary
heading: CORAL
text: (1) (Biology) Marine coelenterates (Madreporaria), solitary or colonial, which form a hard external covering of calcium compounds or other materials. The corals which form large REEFS are limited to warm, shallow waters, while those forming solitary, minute growths may be found in colder waters to great depths. (2) (Geology) The concretion of coral polyps, composed almost wholly of calcium carbonate, forming reefs and tree-like and globular masses. May also include calcareous algae and other organisms producing calcareous secretions, such as bryozoans and hydrozoans.

title: Glossary
heading: CORAL REEF
text: A coral-algal mound or ridge of in-place coral colonies and skeletal fragments, carbonate sand, and organicallysecreted calcium carbonate. A coral reef is built up around a wave-resistant framework, usually of older coral colonies.

title: Glossary
heading: CORE
text: (1) A cylindrical sample extracted from a beach or seabed to investigate the types and depths of sediment layers. (2) An inner, often much less permeable portion of a BREAKWATER or BARRIER BEACH

title: Glossary
heading: CORIOLIS EFFECT
text: Force due to the Earth's rotation, capable of generating currents. It causes moving bodies to be deflected to the right in the Northern Hemisphere and to the left in the Southern Hemisphere. The "force" is proportional to the speed and latitude of the moving object. It is zero at the equator and maximum at the poles.

title: Glossary
heading: COVE
text: A small, sheltered recess in a COAST, often inside a larger EMBAYMENT.

title: Glossary
heading: COVER LAYER
text: The outer layer used in a rubble system as protection against external hydraulic loads

title: Glossary
heading: CREEK
text: (1) A stream, less predominant than a river, and generally tributary to a river. (2) A small tidal channel through a coastal MARSH.

title: Glossary
heading: CREEP
text: Very slow, continuous downslope movement of soil or debris.

title: Glossary
heading: CRENULATE
text: An indented or wavy shoreline beach form, with the regular seaward- pointing parts rounded rather than sharp, as in the cuspate type.

title: Glossary
heading: CREST
text: Highest point on a beach face, BREAKWATER, or SEAWALL.

title: Glossary
heading: CREST LENGTH, WAVE
text: The length of a wave along its crest. Sometimes called CREST WIDTH.

title: Glossary
heading: CREST OF WAVE
text: (1) the highest part of a wave. (2) That part of the wave above still-water level. (See Figure II-1-4.)

title: Glossary
heading: CREST OF BERM
text: The seaward limit of a berm. Also called BERM EDGE. (See Figure A-1.)

title: Glossary
heading: CREST WIDTH, WAVE
text: See CREST LENGTH, WAVE.

title: Glossary
heading: CROSS-BEDDING
text: An arrangement of relatively thin layers of rock inclined at an angle to the more nearly horizontal BEDDING PLANES of the larger rock unit. Also referred to as cross-stratification.

title: Glossary
heading: CROSS-SHORE
text: Perpendicular to the SHORELINE.

title: Glossary
heading: CROWN WALL
text: Concrete superstructure on a rubble mound.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT
text: (1) The flowing of water, or other liquid or gas. (2) That portion of a stream of water which is moving with a velocity much greater than the average or in which the progress of the water is principally concentrated. (3) Ocean currents can be classified in a number of different ways. Some important types include the following: (1) Periodic - due to the effect of the tides; such Currents may be rotating rather than having a simple back and forth motion. The currents accompanying tides are known as tidal currents; (2) Temporary - due to seasonal winds; (3) Permanent or ocean - constitute a part of the general ocean circulation. The term DRIFT CURRENT is often applied to a slow broad movement of the oceanic water; (4) Nearshore - caused principally by waves breaking along a shore.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, COASTAL
text: One of the offshore currents flowing generally parallel to the shoreline in the deeper water beyond and near the surf zone; these are not related genetically to waves and resulting surf, but may be related to tides, winds, or distribution of mass.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, DRIFT
text: A broad, shallow, slow-moving ocean or lake current. Opposite of CURRENT, STREAM.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, EBB
text: The tidal current away from shore or down a tidal stream. Usually associated with the decrease in the height of the tide.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, EDDY
text: See EDDY.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, FEEDER
text: Any of the parts of the nearshore current system that flow parallel to shore before converging and forming the neck of the RIP CURRENT.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, FLOOD
text: The tidal current toward shore or up a tidal stream. Usually associated with the increase in the height of the tide.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, INSHORE
text: See INSHORE CURRENT.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, LITTORAL
text: Any current in the littoral zone caused primarily by wave action; e.g., LONGSHORE CURRENT, RIP CURRENT. See also CURRENT, NEARSHORE.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, LONGSHORE
text: The littoral current in the breaker zone moving essentially parallel to the shore, usually generated by waves breaking at an angle to the shoreline.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT METER
text: An instrument for measuring the velocity of a current

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, NEARSHORE
text: A current in the NEARSHORE ZONE. (See Figure A-1)

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, OFFSHORE
text: See OFFSHORE CURRENT.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, PERIODIC
text: See CURRENT, TIDAL.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, PERMANENT
text: See PERMANENT CURRENT.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, RIP
text: See RIP CURRENT.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, STREAM
text: A narrow, deep, and swift ocean current, as the Gulf Stream. CURRENT, DRIFT.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT SYSTEM, NEARSHORE
text: See NEARSHORE CURRENT SYSTEM.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT, TIDAL
text: The alternating horizontal movement of water associated with the rise and fall of the tide caused by the astronomical tide-producing forces. Also CURRENT, PERIODIC. See also CURRENT, FLOOD and CURRENT, EBB.

title: Glossary
heading: CURRENT-REFRACTION
text: Process by which wave velocity, height, and direction are affected by a current

title: Glossary
heading: CUSP
text: One of a series of short ridges on the FORESHORE separated by crescent-shaped troughs spaced at more or less regular intervals. Between these cusps are hollows. The cusps are spaced at somewhat uniform distances along beaches. They represent a combination of constructive and destructive processes. Also BEACH CUSP. (See Figure III-2-23.)

title: Glossary
heading: CUSPATE BAR
text: A crescent-shaped bar uniting with the shore at each end. It may be formed by a single spit growing from shore and then turning back to again meet the shore, or by two spits growing from the shore and uniting to form a bar of sharply cuspate form.

title: Glossary
heading: CUSPATE SPIT
text: The spit that forms in the lee of a shoal or offshore feature (BREAKWATER, island, rock outcrop) by waves that are refracted and/or diffracted around the offshore feature. It may eventually grow into a TOMBOLO linking the feature to the mainland.

title: Glossary
heading: CUTTERHEAD DREDGE
text: A hydraulic dredge that uses a rotating steel head consisting of hardened cutting blades and a backing ring to dislodge bottom material. The head is mounted at the suction entrance of the hydraulic pipeline, and fluidized material is picked up by suction and carried away through the pipe.

title: Glossary
heading: CYCLOIDAL WAVE
text: A steep, symmetrical wave whose crest forms an angle of 120 degrees and whose form is that of a cycloid. A trochoidal wave of maximum steepness. See also TROCHOIDAL WAVE.

title: Glossary
heading: CYCLONE
text: A system of winds that rotates about a center of low atmospheric pressure. Rotation is clockwise in the Southern Hemisphere and anti-clockwise in the Northern Hemisphere. In the Indian Ocean, the term refers to the powerful storms called HURRICANES in the Atlantic. D

title: Glossary
heading: DAM
text: Structure built in rivers or estuaries, basically to separate water at both sides and/or to retain water at one side.

title: Glossary
heading: DATUM
text: Any permanent line, plane or surface used as a reference datum to which elevations are referred.

title: Glossary
heading: DATUM, CHART
text: See CHART DATUM.

title: Glossary
heading: DATUM, PLANE
text: The horizontal plane to which soundings, ground elevations, or water surface elevations are referred. Also REFERENCE PLANE. The plane is called a TIDAL DATUM when defined by a certain phase of the tide. The following datums are ordinarily used on hydrographic charts: MEAN LOW WATER--Atlantic coast (U. S.), Argentina, Sweden, and Norway. MEAN LOWER LOW WATER--Pacific coast (U. S.). MEAN LOW WATER SPRINGS--United Kingdom, Germany, Italy, Brazil, and Chile. LOW WATER DATUM--Great Lakes (U. S. and Canada). LOWEST LOW WATER SPRINGS--Portugal. LOW WATER INDIAN SPRINGS--India and Japan (See INDIAN TIDE PLANE). LOWEST LOW WATER--France, Spain, and Greece. A common datum used on United States topographic maps is MEAN SEA LEVEL. See also BENCH MARK.

title: Glossary
heading: DAVIDSON CURRENT
text: Deep-ocean boundary current off the west coast of the U.S. which brings warmer, saltier, low oxygen, high phosphate equatorial type water from low to high latitudes.

title: Glossary
heading: DEBRIS LINE
text: A line near the limit of storm wave uprush marking the landward limit of debris deposits.

title: Glossary
heading: DECAY AREA
text: Area of relative CALM through which waves travel after emerging from the generating area.

title: Glossary
heading: DECAY DISTANCE
text: The distance waves travel after leaving the generating area (FETCH).

title: Glossary
heading: DECAY OF WAVES
text: The change waves undergo after they leave a generating area (FETCH) and pass through a calm, or region of lighter winds. In the process of decay, the significant wave height decreases and the significant wavelength increases.

title: Glossary
heading: DEEP WATER
text: Water so deep that surface waves are little affected by the ocean bottom. Generally, water deeper than one-half the surface wavelength is considered deep water. Compare SHALLOW WATER.

title: Glossary
heading: DEEP WATER WAVES
text: A wave in water the depth of which is greater than one-half the WAVE LENGTH.

title: Glossary
heading: DEFLATION
text: The removal of loose material from a beach or other land surface by wind action.

title: Glossary
heading: DEGRADATION
text: The geologic process by means of which various parts of the surface of the earth are worn away and their general level lowered, by the action of wind and water.

title: Glossary
heading: DELTA
text: (1) An ALLUVIAL DEPOSIT, usually triangular or semi-circular, at the mouth of a river or stream. The delta is normally built up only where there is no tidal or current action capable of removing the sediment at the same rate as it is deposited, and hence the delta builds forward from the coastline. (2) A TIDAL DELTA is a similar deposit at the mouth of a tidal INLET, the result of TIDAL CURRENTS that flow in and out of the inlet.

title: Glossary
heading: DELTA PLAIN
text: The nearly-level surface composing the landward portion of a large DELTA.

title: Glossary
heading: DENSITY
text: Mass (in kg) per unit of volume of a substance; $kg/m^{3}.$ For pure water, the density is 1000 $kg/m^{3},$ for seawater the density is usually more. Density increases with increasing salinity, and decreases with increasing temperature. More information can be found in "properties of seawater". For stone and sand, usually a density of 2600 $kg/m^{3}$ is assumed. Concrete is less dense, in the order of 2400 $kg/m^{3}.$ Some types of basalt may reach 2800 $kg/m^{3}.$ For sand, including the voids, one may use 1600 $kg/m^{3},$ while mud often has a density of 1100 - 1200 $kg/m^{3}.$

title: Glossary
heading: DENSITY CURRENT
text: Phenomenon of relative flow within water due to difference in density. For example, the salt-water wedge is a density current, as is a volcanic nuée ardente .

title: Glossary
heading: DENSITY STRATIFICATION
text: The lateral expansion of a sediment plume as it moves out of the distributary mouth, where salt and fresh water mix. This is most likely to occur where the speed of the river flow is moderate to low and the distributary mouth is relatively deep.

title: Glossary
heading: DENSITY-DRIVEN CIRCULATION
text: Variations in salinity create variations in density in estuaries. These variations in density create horizontal pressure gradients, which drive estuarine circulation.

title: Glossary
heading: DEPRESSION
text: A general term signifying any depressed or lower area in the ocean floor.

title: Glossary
heading: DEPTH
text: The vertical distance from a specified datum to the sea floor.

title: Glossary
heading: DEPTH CONTOUR
text: See CONTOUR, also ISOBATH.

title: Glossary
heading: DEPTH, CONTROLLING
text: See CONTROLLING DEPTH.

title: Glossary
heading: DEPTH FACTOR
text: See SHOALING COEFFICIENT.

title: Glossary
heading: DEPTH OF BREAKING
text: The still-water depth at the point where the wave breaks. Also BREAKER DEPTH.

title: Glossary
heading: DERRICK STONE
text: See STONE, DERRICK.

title: Glossary
heading: DESIGN HURRICANE
text: See HYPOTHETICAL HURRICANE.

title: Glossary
heading: DESIGN STORM
text: A hypothetical extreme storm whose waves coastal protection structures will often be designed to withstand. The severity of the storm (i.e. return period) is chosen in view of the acceptable level of risk of damage or failure. A DESIGN STORM consists of a DESIGN WAVE condition, a design water level and a duration.

title: Glossary
heading: DESIGN WAVE
text: In the design of HARBORS, harbor works, etc., the type or types of waves selected as having the characteristics against which protection is desired.

title: Glossary
heading: DESIGN WAVE CONDITION
text: Usually an extreme wave condition with a specified return period used in the design of coastal works.

title: Glossary
heading: DETACHED BREAKWATER
text: A BREAKWATER without any SUBAERIAL connection to the shore.

title: Glossary
heading: DETRITUS
text: Small fragments of rock which have been worn or broken away from a mass by the action of water or waves.

title: Glossary
heading: DIFFERENTIAL EROSION / WEATHERING
text: These features develop in rocks which have varying resistance to the agencies of erosion and/or weathering so that parts of the rock are removed at greater rates than others. A typical example is the removal of soft beds from between harder beds in a series of sedimentary rocks. The term may be applied to any size of feature, from small-scale 'etching' to the regional development of hills and valleys controlled by hard and soft rocks.

title: Glossary
heading: DIFFRACTION (of water waves)
text: The phenomenon by which energy is transmitted laterally along a wave crest. When a part of a train of waves is interrupted by a barrier, such as a BREAKWATER, the effect of diffraction is manifested by propagation of waves into the sheltered region within the barrier's geometric shadow. (See Figure II-7-2)

title: Glossary
heading: DIFFRACTION COEFFICIENT
text: Ratio of diffracted wave height to DEEPWATER wave height.

title: Glossary
heading: DIFFUSION
text: The distributing or spreading-out of molecules, atoms, or substances into a vacuum, fluid, or porous medium so that the concentration equalizes in all parts of the system

title: Glossary
heading: DIKE
text: Earth structure along sea or river in order to protect low lands from flooding by high water; dikes along rivers are sometimes called LEVEES. Sometimes written as DYKE

title: Glossary
heading: DISCHARGE
text: The volume of water per unit of time flowing along a pipe or channel.

title: Glossary
heading: DISPERSION
text: (1) Pattern of geographic distribution of individuals within a species. (2) Distortion of the shape of a seismic wave train or ocean wave train because of variations of velocity with frequency.

title: Glossary
heading: DISTRIBUTARIES
text: Multiple branches of a river.

title: Glossary
heading: DITCH
text: A channel to convey water for irrigation or drainage.

title: Glossary
heading: DIURNAL
text: Having a period or cycle of approximately one TIDAL DAY.

title: Glossary
heading: DIURNAL CURRENT
text: The type of tidal current having only one FLOOD and one EBB period in the tidal day. A ROTARY CURRENT is diurnal if it changes its direction through all points of the compass once each tidal day.

title: Glossary
heading: DIURNAL INEQUALITY
text: The difference in height of the two high waters or of the two low waters of each day. Also, the difference in velocity between the two daily flood or EBB CURRENTS of each day.

title: Glossary
heading: DIURNAL TIDE
text: A tide with one high water and one low water in a tidal day. (See Figure II-5-16)

title: Glossary
heading: DIVERGENCE
text: (1) In refraction phenomena, the increasing of distance between orthogonals in the direction of wave travel. Denotes an area of decreasing wave height and energy concentration. (2) In wind-setup phenomena, the decrease in setup observed under that which would occur in an equivalent rectangular basin of uniform depth, caused by changes in planform or depth. Also the increase in basin width or depth causing such decrease in setup.

title: Glossary
heading: DIVERGING WAVE
text: Waves that move obliquely out from a vessel's sailing line. (See Figure II-7-40)

title: Glossary
heading: DIVERSION CHANNEL
text: A waterway used to divert water from its natural course. The term is generally applied to a temporary arrangement e.g. to by-pass water around a dam site during construction.

title: Glossary
heading: DOCK
text: The slip or waterway between two piers, or cut into the land, for the reception of ships.

title: Glossary
heading: DOLPHIN
text: A cluster of piles.

title: Glossary
heading: DOWNDRIFT
text: The direction of predominant movement of littoral materials.

title: Glossary
heading: DOWNSTREAM
text: Along coasts with obliquely approaching waves there is a longshore (wave-driven) current. For this current, one can define an upstream and a DOWNSTREAM direction. For example, on a beach with an orientation west-east, the sea is to the north. Suppose the waves come from NW, then the current flows from West to East. Here, UPSTREAM is west of the observer, and east is downstream of the observer.

title: Glossary
heading: DOWNWELLING
text: A downward movement (sinking) of surface water caused by onshore Ekman transport, converging CURRENTS, or when a water mass becomes more dense than the surrounding water.

title: Glossary
heading: DRAINAGE BASIN
text: Total area drained by a stream and its tributaries.

title: Glossary
heading: DREDGED CHANNEL
text: An artificially maintained sea lane extending from an inland water body into the marginal sea to accomodate vessel traffic through coastal shallows.

title: Glossary
heading: DREDGED MATERIAL PLACEMENT SITE
text: Designated area for dredged material placement. In the United States, designated areas must be coordinated with the Environmental Protection Agency and resource agencies such as the U. S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the National marine Fisheries Service for environmental compliance and with local interests for capacity and acceptability.

title: Glossary
heading: DREDGING
text: The practice of excavating or displacing the bottom or shoreline of a water body. Dredging can be accomplished with mechanical or hydraulic machines. Most is done to maintain channel depths or berths for navigational purposes; other dredging is for shellfish harvesting, for cleanup of polluted sediments, and for placement of sand on beaches.

title: Glossary
heading: DRIFT (noun)
text: (1) Sometimes used as a short form for LITTORAL DRIFT. (2) The speed at which a current runs. (3) Floating material deposited on a beach (driftwood). (4) A deposit of a continental ice sheet; e.g., a DRUMLIN.

title: Glossary
heading: DRIFT CURRENT
text: A broad, shallow, slow-moving ocean or lake current.

title: Glossary
heading: DRIFT SECTOR
text: A particular reach of marine shore in which LITTORAL DRIFT may occur without significant interruption, and which contain any and all natural sources of such drift, and also any accretion shore forms accreted by such drift.

title: Glossary
heading: DROMOND
text: A large medieval fast-sailing galley or cutter.

title: Glossary
heading: DROWNED COAST
text: A shore with long, narrow channels, implying that subsidence of the coast has transformed the lower portions of river valleys into tidal estuaries.

title: Glossary
heading: DRUMLIN
text: A low, smoothly-rounded, elongate hill of compact glacial till built under the margin of the ice and shaped by its flow.

title: Glossary
heading: DRYING BEACH
text: That part of the beach which is uncovered by water (e.g. at low tide). Sometimes referred to as 'SUBAERIAL' beach.

title: Glossary
heading: DUNES
text: (1) Ridges or mounds of loose, wind-blown material, usually sand. (See Figure IV-2-11.) (2) Bed forms smaller than bars but larger than ripples that are out of phase with any water-surface gravity waves associated with them.

title: Glossary
heading: DURABILITY
text: The ability of a rock to retain its physical and mechanical properties (i.e. resist degradation) in engineering service.

title: Glossary
heading: DURATION
text: In wave forecasting, the length of time the wind blows in nearly the same direction over the FETCH (generating area).

title: Glossary
heading: DURATION, MINIMUM
text: The time necessary for steady-state wave conditions to develop for a given wind velocity over a given fetch length.

title: Glossary
heading: DURATION OF EBB
text: The interval of time in which a tidal current is ebbing, determined from the middle of the slack waters.

title: Glossary
heading: DURATION OF FALL
text: The interval from high water to low water.

title: Glossary
heading: DURATION OF FLOOD
text: The interval of time in which a tidal current is flooding, determined from the middle of slack waters.

title: Glossary
heading: DURATION OF RISE
text: The interval from low water to high water.

title: Glossary
heading: DYNAMIC EQUILIBRIUM
text: Short term morphological changes that do not affect the morphology over a long period.

title: Glossary
heading: DYNAMIC VISCOSITY
text: In fluid dynamics, the ratio between the shear stress acting along any plane between neighboring fluid elements and the rate of deformation of the velocity gradient perpendicular to this plane. E

title: Glossary
heading: EAGER
text: See BORE.

title: Glossary
heading: EBB
text: Period when tide level is falling; often taken to mean the ebb current which occurs during this period.

title: Glossary
heading: EBB CURRENT
text: The movement of a tidal current away from shore or down a tidal stream. In the semidiurnal type of reversing current, the terms greater ebb and lesser ebb are applied respectively to the ebb currents of greater and lesser velocity of each day. The terms of maximum ebb and minimum ebb are applied to the maximum and minimum velocities of a continuously running ebb current , the velocity alternately increasing and decreasing without coming to a slack or reversing. The expression maximum ebb is also applicable to any ebb current at the time of greatest velocity.

title: Glossary
heading: EBB INTERVAL
text: The interval between the transit of the moon over the meridian of a place and the time of the following strength of ebb.

title: Glossary
heading: EBB SHIELD
text: High, landward margin of a flood-tidal shoal that helps divert ebb-tide currents around the shoal.

title: Glossary
heading: EBB STRENGTH
text: The EBB CURRENT at the time of maximum velocity.

title: Glossary
heading: EBB TIDAL DELTA
text: The bulge of sand formed at the seaward mouth of TIDAL INLETS as a result of interaction between tidal currents and waves. Also called inlet-associated bars and estuary entrance shoals.

title: Glossary
heading: EBB TIDE
text: The period of tide between high water and the succeeding low water; a falling tide. (See Figure II-5-16.)

title: Glossary
heading: ECHO SOUNDER
text: An electronic instrument used to determine the depth of water by measuring the time interval between the emission of a sonic or ultrasonic signal and the return of its echo from the bottom.

title: Glossary
heading: ECOSYSTEM
text: The living organisms and the nonliving environment interacting in a given area, encompassing the relationships between biological, geochemical, and geophysical systems.

title: Glossary
heading: EDDY
text: A circular movement of water formed on the side of a main current. Eddies may be created at points where the main stream passes projecting obstructions or where two adjacent currents flow counter to each other.

title: Glossary
heading: EDDY CURRENT
text: See EDDY.

title: Glossary
heading: EDGE WAVE
text: An ocean wave parallel to a coast, with crests normal to the shoreline. An edge wave may be STANDING or PROGRESSIVE. Its height diminishes rapidly seaward and is negligible at a distance of one wavelength offshore.

title: Glossary
heading: EKMAN TRANSPORT
text: Resultant flow at right angles to and to the right of the wind direction (in the northern hemisphere) referred to as UPWELLING and DOWNWELLING.

title: Glossary
heading: ELEVATION
text: The vertical distance from mean sea level or other established datum plane to a point on the earth's surface; height above sea level. Although sea floor elevation below msl should be marked as a negative value, many charts show positive numerals for water depth.

title: Glossary
heading: EL NIÑO
text: Warm equatorial water which flows southward along the coast of Peru and Ecuador during February and March of certain years. It is caused by poleward motions of air and unusual water temperature patterns in the Pacific Ocean, which cause coastal downwelling, leading to the reversal in the normal north-flowing cold coastal currents. During many El Niño years, storms, rainfall, and other meteorological phenomena in the Western Hemisphere are measurably different than during non- El Niño years.

title: Glossary
heading: ELUTRIATION
text: The process by which a granular material can be sorted into its constituent particle sizes by means of a moving stream of fluid (usually air or water). Elutriators are extensively used in studies of sediments for determining Particle size distribution. Under certain circumstances wind, rivers and streams may act as elutriating agents.

title: Glossary
heading: EMBANKMENT
text: Fill material, usually earth or rock, placed with sloping sides and with a length greater than its height. Usually an embankment is wider than a dike.

title: Glossary
heading: EMBAYMENT
text: An indentation in the shore forming an open bay.

title: Glossary
heading: EMERGENT COAST
text: A coast in which land formerly under water has recently been exposed above sea level, either by uplift of the land or by a drop in sea level.

title: Glossary
heading: ENDEMIC
text: Native or confined to a specific geographic area.

title: Glossary
heading: ENERGY COEFFICIENT
text: The ratio of the energy in a wave per unit crest length transmitted forward with the wave at a point in shallow water to the energy in a wave per unit crest length transmitted forward with the wave in deep water. On refraction diagrams this is equal to the ratio of the distance between a pair of orthogonals at a selected shallow-water point to the distance between the same pair of orthogonals in deep water. Also the square of the REFRACTION COEFFICIENT.

title: Glossary
heading: ENTRANCE
text: The avenue of access or opening to a navigable channel or inlet.

title: Glossary
heading: EOLIAN (also AEOLIAN)
text: Pertaining to the wind, esp. used with deposits such as loess and dune sand, and sedimentary structures like windformed ripple marks.

title: Glossary
heading: EOLIAN SANDS
text: Sediments of sand size or smaller which have been transported by winds. They may be recognized in marine deposits off desert coasts by the greater angularity of the grains compared with waterborne particles.

title: Glossary
heading: EQUATORIAL CURRENTS
text: (1) Ocean currents flowing westerly near the equator. There are two such currents in both the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans. The one to the north of the equator is called the North Equatorial Current and the one to the south is called the South Equatorial Current. Between these two currents there is an easterly flowing stream known as the Equatorial Countercurrent. (2) Tidal currents occurring semimonthly as a result of the moon being over the equator. At these times the tendency of the moon to produce DIURNAL INEQUALITY in the current is at a minimum.

title: Glossary
heading: EQUATORIAL TIDES
text: Tides occurring semimonthly as the result of the moon being over the equator. At these times the tendency of the moon to produce a DIURNAL INEQUALITY in the tide is at a minimum.

title: Glossary
heading: EROSION
text: The wearing away of land by the action of natural forces. On a beach, the carrying away of beach material by wave action, tidal currents, littoral currents, or by deflation.

title: Glossary
heading: ESCARPMENT
text: A more or less continuous line of cliffs or steep slopes facing in one general direction which are caused by erosion or faulting. Also SCARP. (See Figure A-1)

title: Glossary
heading: ESTUARY
text: (1) The part of a river that is affected by tides. (2) The region near a river mouth in which the fresh water of the river mixes with the salt water of the sea and which received both fluvial and littoral sediment influx.

title: Glossary
heading: EUSTATIC SEA LEVEL CHANGE
text: Change in the relative volume of the world's ocean basins and the total amount of ocean water.

title: Glossary
heading: EYE
text: In meteorology, usually the "eye of the storm" (hurricane): the roughly circular area of comparatively light winds and fair weather found at the center of a severe tropical cyclone. F

title: Glossary
heading: FAIRWAY
text: The parts of a waterway that are open and unobstructed for navigation. The main traveled part of a waterway; a marine thoroughfare.

title: Glossary
heading: FAR-INFRAGRAVITY
text: The frequency band (nominally 0.001 - 0.02 Hz) occupied by SHEAR INSTABILITIES of the longshore current. This band falls both below and in the lower part of the Infragravity band occupied by Infragravity waves.

title: Glossary
heading: FATHOM
text: A unit of measurement used for soundings equal to 1.83 meters (6 feet).

title: Glossary
heading: FATHOMETER
text: The copyrighted trademark for a type of ECHO SOUNDER.

title: Glossary
heading: FAULT
text: A fracture in rock along which there has been an observable amount of displacement. Faults are rarely single planar units; normally they occur as parallel to sub-parallel sets of planes along which movement has taken place to a greater or lesser extent. Such sets are called fault or fracture-zones.

title: Glossary
heading: FAUNA
text: The entire group of animals found in an area.

title: Glossary
heading: FEEDER BEACH
text: An artificially widened beach serving to nourish downdrift beaches by natural littoral currents or forces.

title: Glossary
heading: FEEDER CURRENT
text: The currents which flow parallel to shore before converging and forming the neck of a RIP CURRENT.

title: Glossary
heading: FEEDER CURRENT
text: See CURRENT, FEEDER.

title: Glossary
heading: FEELING BOTTOM
text: The initial action of a deepwater wave, in response to the bottom, upon running into shoal water.

title: Glossary
heading: FETCH
text: The area in which SEAS are generated by a wind having a fairly constant direction and speed. Sometimes used synonymously with FETCH LENGTH. Also GENERATING AREA.

title: Glossary
heading: FETCH LENGTH
text: The horizontal distance (in the direction of the wind) over which a wind generates seas or creates a WIND SETUP.

title: Glossary
heading: FETCH-LIMITED
text: Situation in which wave energy (or wave height) is limited by the size of the wave generation area (fetch).

title: Glossary
heading: FILTER
text: Intermediate layer, preventing fine materials of an underlayer from being washed through the voids of an upper layer.

title: Glossary
heading: FIORD (FJORD)
text: A narrow, deep, steep-walled inlet of the sea, usually formed by entrance of the sea into a deep glacial trough.

title: Glossary
heading: FIRTH
text: A narrow arm of the sea; also, the opening of a river into the sea.

title: Glossary
heading: FLOOD
text: (1) Period when tide level is rising; often taken to mean the flood current which occurs during this period (2) A flow beyond the carrying capacity of a channel.

title: Glossary
heading: FLOOD CHANNEL
text: Channel located on ebb-tidal shoal that carries the flood tide over the tidal flat into the back bay or lagoon.

title: Glossary
heading: FLOOD CURRENT
text: The movement of a tidal current toward the shore or up a tidal stream. In the semidiurnal type of reversing current, the terms greater flood and lesser flood are applied respectively to the flood currents of greater and lesser velocity each day. The terms maximum flood and minimum flood are applied to the maximum and minimum velocities of a flood current the velocity of which alternately increases and decreases without coming to slack or reversing. The expression maximum flood is also applicable to any flood current at the time of greatest velocity.

title: Glossary
heading: FLOOD GATE
text: A gravity outlet fitted with vertically-hinged doors, opening if the inner water level is higher than the outer water level, so that drainage takes place during low water.

title: Glossary
heading: FLOOD INTERVAL
text: The interval between the transit of the moon over the meridian of a place and the time of the following flood.

title: Glossary
heading: FLOOD MARK
text: Proof of any kind on the shoreline, or on structures like bridge abutments, used to determine the highest level attained by the water surface during the flood (note: the height of the flood mark usually includes the wave run-up).

title: Glossary
heading: FLOOD PLAIN
text: 1) A flat tract of land bordering a river, mainly in its lower reaches, and consisting of ALLUVIUM deposited by the river. It is formed by the sweeping of the meander belts downstream, thus widening the valley, the sides of which may become some kilometers apart. In time of flood, when the river overflows its banks, sediment is deposited along the valley banks and plains. (2) Synonymous with 100-year floodplain . The land area susceptible to being inundated by stream derived waters with a 1 percent chance of being equaled or exceeded in any given year.

title: Glossary
heading: FLOOD RAMP
text: Seaward-dipping sand platform dominated by flood-tidal currents, located on ebb-tidal shoal near the opening to the inlet.

title: Glossary
heading: FLOOD ROUTING
text: The determination of the attenuating effect of storage on a river-flood passing through a valley by reason of a feature acting as control (e.g. a reservoir with a spillway capacity less than the flood inflow, or the widening or narrowing of a valley).

title: Glossary
heading: FLOOD TIDAL DELTA
text: The bulge of sand formed at the landward mouth of TIDAL INLETS as a result of flow expansion.

title: Glossary
heading: FLOOD TIDE
text: The period of tide between low water and the succeeding high water; a rising tide. (See Figure II-5-16.)

title: Glossary
heading: FLOOD WALL, SPLASH WALL
text: Wall, retired from the seaward edge of the seawall crest, to prevent water from flowing onto the land behind.

title: Glossary
heading: FLORA
text: The entire group of plants found in an area.

title: Glossary
heading: FLUVIAL
text: Of or pertaining to rivers; produced by the action of a river or stream (e.g., fluvial sediment).

title: Glossary
heading: FLUSHING TIME
text: The time required to replace all the water in an ESTUARY, HARBOR, etc., by action of current and tide.

title: Glossary
heading: FOAM LINE
text: (1) The front of a wave as it advances shoreward, after it has broken. (2) Lines of foam such as those which move around the head of a RIP.

title: Glossary
heading: FOLLOWING WIND
text: Generally, the same as a tailwind; in wave forecasting, wind blowing in the direction of ocean-wave advance.

title: Glossary
heading: FOREDUNE
text: The front DUNE immediately behind the backshore.

title: Glossary
heading: FORERUNNER
text: Low, long-period ocean SWELL which commonly precedes the main swell from a distant storm, especially a tropical cyclone.

title: Glossary
heading: FORESHORE
text: The part of the shore, lying between the crest of the seaward berm (or upper limit of wave wash at high tide) and the ordinary low-water mark, that is ordinarily traversed by the uprush and backrush of the waves as the tides rise and fall. See BEACH FACE. (See Figure A-1)

title: Glossary
heading: FORE REEF
text: The seaward side of a REEF (usually coral); in places a steep slope covered with reef talus.

title: Glossary
heading: FORWARD SPEED (hurricane)
text: Rate of movement (propagation) of the hurricane eye in meters per second, knots, or miles per hour.

title: Glossary
heading: FREEBOARD
text: At a given time, the vertical distance between the water level and the top of the structure. On a ship, the distance from the waterline to main deck or gunwale.

title: Glossary
heading: FRINGING ISLANDS
text: A series of islands that fringe, or mask, a mainland coast often known as BARRIER ISLANDS. The waters landward of such islands are sometimes treated as high seas enclaves or as inland waters.

title: Glossary
heading: FRINGING REEF
text: A coral REEF attached directly to an insular or continental shore. There may be a shallow channel or lagoon between the reef and the adjacent mainland.

title: Glossary
heading: FRONT OF THE FETCH
text: In wave forecasting, the end of the generating area toward which the wind is blowing.

title: Glossary
heading: FROUDE NUMBER
text: The dimensionless ratio of the inertial force to the force of gravity for a given fluid flow. It may be given as $F {r}$ = V /Lg where V is a characteristic velocity, L is a characteristic length, and g the acceleration of gravity - or as the square root of this number.

title: Glossary
heading: FULLY-DEVELOPED SEA
text: The waves that form when wind blows for a sufficient period of time across the open ocean. The waves of a fully developed sea have the maximum height possible for a given wind speed, FETCH and duration of wind. G

title: Glossary
heading: GABION
text: (1) Steel wire-mesh basket to hold stones or crushed rock to protect a bank or bottom from erosion. (2) Structures composed of masses of rocks, rubble or masonry held tightly together usually by wire mesh so as to form blocks or walls. Sometimes used on heavy erosion areas to retard wave action or as a foundation for BREAKWATERS or JETTIES.

title: Glossary
heading: GALE
text: A wind between a strong breeze and a storm. A continuous wind blowing in degrees of moderate, fresh, strong, or whole gale and varying in velocity from 28 to 47 nautical miles per hour (see BEAUFORT SCALE).

title: Glossary
heading: GAUGE (GAGE)
text: Instrument for measuring the water level relative to a datum or for measuring other parameters.

title: Glossary
heading: GENERATING AREA
text: In wave forecasting, the continuous area of water surface over which the wind blows in nearly a constant direction. Sometimes used synonymously with FETCH LENGTH. Also FETCH.

title: Glossary
heading: GEOGRAPHICAL INFORMATION SYSTEM (GIS)
text: Database of information which is geographically referenced, usually with an associated visualization system.

title: Glossary
heading: GEOGRAPHIC MILE
text: Unit of linear measure equal to one minute of latitude at the equator. See NAUTICAL MILE.

title: Glossary
heading: GEOMETRIC MEAN DIAMETER
text: The diameter equivalent of the arithmetic mean of the logarithmic frequency distribution. In the analysis of beach sands, it is taken as that grain diameter determined graphically by the intersection of a straight line through selected boundary sizes, (generally points on the distribution curve where 16 and 84 percent of the sample is coarser by weight) and a vertical line through the median diameter of the sample.

title: Glossary
heading: GEOMETRIC SHADOW
text: In wave diffraction theory, the area outlined by drawing straight lines paralleling the direction of wave approach through the extremities of a protective structure. It differs from the actual protected area to the extent that the diffraction and refraction effects modify the wave pattern.

title: Glossary
heading: GEOMORPHOLOGY
