text: (1) That branch of physical geography which deals with the form of the Earth, the general configuration of its surface, the distribution of the land, water, etc. (2) The investigation of the history of geologic changes through the interpretation of topographic forms.

title: Glossary
heading: GEOPHYSICS
text: The study of the physical characteristics and properties of the earth, usually employing quantitative physical methods.

title: Glossary
heading: GEOTEXTILE
text: A synthetic fabric which may be woven or non-woven used as a filter.

title: Glossary
heading: GLACIER
text: A large body of ice moving slowly down a slope of valley or spreading outward on a land surface (e.g., Greenland, Antarctica) and surviving from year to year.

title: Glossary
heading: GLACIO-ISOSTACY
text: The state of hydrostatic equilibrium of the earth's crust as influenced by the weight of glacier ice.

title: Glossary
heading: GLOBAL POSITIONING SYSTEM (GPS)
text: A navigational and positioning system developed by the U.S. Department of Defense, by which the location of a position on or above the Earth can be determined by a special receiver at that point interpreting signals received simultaneously from several of a constellation of special satellites.

title: Glossary
heading: GORGE
text: (1) The deepest portion of an inlet, the THROAT. (2) A narrow, deep valley with nearly vertical rock walls.

title: Glossary
heading: GRADED BEDDING
text: An arrangement of particle sizes within a single bed, with coarse grains at the bottom of the bed and progressively finer grains toward the top of the bed.

title: Glossary
heading: GRADIENT
text: (1) A measure of slope (soil- or water-surface) in meters of rise or fall per meter of horizontal distance. (2) More general, a change of a value per unit of distance, e.g. the gradient in longshore transport causes erosion or accretion. (3) With reference to winds or currents, the rate of increase or decrease in speed, usually in the vertical; or the curve that represents this rate.

title: Glossary
heading: GRADING
text: Distribution, with regard to size or weight, of individual stones within a bulk volume; heavy, light and fine grading are distinguished.

title: Glossary
heading: GRADUAL CLOSURE METHOD
text: Method in which the final closure gap in a dam is closed gradually either by the vertical or horizontal closure method; this in contradiction with a sudden closure.

title: Glossary
heading: GRANULAR FILTER
text: Band of granular material which is incorporated in an embankment, dam, dike, or bottom protection and is graded so as to allow seepage to flow across or down the filter zone without causing the migration of the material adjacent to the filter.

title: Glossary
heading: GRAVEL
text: Unconsolidated natural accumulation of rounded rock fragments coarser than sand but finer than pebbles (2-4 mm diameter).

title: Glossary
heading: GRAVITY WAVE
text: A wave whose velocity of propagation is controlled primarily by gravity. Water waves more than 5 cm long are considered gravity waves. Waves longer than 2.5 cm and shorter than 5 cm are in an indeterminate zone between CAPILLARY and GRAVITY WAVES. See RIPPLE.

title: Glossary
heading: GROIN (British, GROYNE)
text: Narrow, roughly shore-normal structure built to reduce longshore currents, and/or to trap and retain littoral material. Most groins are of timber or rock and extend from a SEAWALL, or the backshore, well onto the foreshore and rarely even further offshore. See T-GROIN, PERMEABLE GROIN, IMPERMEABLE GROIN.

title: Glossary
heading: GROIN BAY
text: The beach compartment between two groins.

title: Glossary
heading: GROIN SYSTEM
text: A series of groins acting together to protect a section of beach. Commonly called a GROIN field.

title: Glossary
heading: GULF
text: A relatively large portion of the ocean or sea extending far into land; the largest of various forms of inlets of the sea (e.g., Gulf of Mexico, Gulf of Aqaba). GUT A tidal stream connecting two larger waterways. H

title: Glossary
heading: HALCOCLINE
text: A zone in which salinity changes rapidly.

title: Glossary
heading: HALF-TIDE LEVEL
text: A plane midway between MEAN HIGH WATER and MEAN LOW WATER, also called MEAN TIDE LEVEL.

title: Glossary
heading: HARBOR (British, HARBOUR)
text: Any protected water area affording a place of safety for vessels. See also PORT. A harbor may be natural or man-made.

title: Glossary
heading: HARBOR OSCILLATION (HARBOR SURGING)
text: The nontidal vertical water movement in a harbor or bay. Usually the vertical motions are low; but when oscillations are excited by a tsunami or storm surge, they may be quite large. Variable winds, air oscillations, or surf beat also may cause oscillations. See SEICHE.

title: Glossary
heading: HARD DEFENSES
text: General term applied to impermeable coastal defense structures of concrete, timber, steel, masonry, etc, which reflect a high proportion of incident wave energy.

title: Glossary
heading: HEAD OF RIP
text: The part of a rip current that has widened out seaward of the breakers. See also CURRENT, RIP; CURRENT, FEEDER; and NECK (RIP).

title: Glossary
heading: HEADLAND (HEAD)
text: (1) A comparatively high promontory with either a CLIFF or steep face extending out into a body of water, such as a sea or lake. An unnamed HEAD is usually called a headland . (2) The section of RIP CURRENT which has widened out seaward of the BREAKERS, also called HEAD OF RIP. (3) Seaward end of BREAKWATER or dam.

title: Glossary
heading: HEADWATER LEVEL
text: The level of water in the reservoir.

title: Glossary
heading: HEAVE
text: (1) The vertical rise or fall of the waves or the sea. (2) The translational movement of a craft parallel to its vertical axis. (3) The net transport of a floating body resulting from wave action.

title: Glossary
heading: HIGH SEAS
text: This term, in municipal and international law, denotes the continuous body of salt water in the world that is navigable in its character and that lies outside territorial waters and maritime belts of the various countries.

title: Glossary
heading: HIGH TIDE, HIGH WATER (HW)
text: The maximum elevation reached by each rising tide. See TIDE. (See Figure II-5-16.)

title: Glossary
heading: HIGH WATER (HW)
text: Maximum height reached by a rising tide. The height may be solely due to the periodic tidal forces or it may have superimposed upon it the effects of prevailing meteorological conditions. Nontechnically, also called the HIGH TIDE.

title: Glossary
heading: HIGH WATER LINE
text: In strictness, the intersection of the plane of mean high water with the shore. The shoreline delineated on the nautical charts of the National Ocean Service is an approximation of the high water line. For specific occurrences, the highest elevation on the shore reached during a storm or rising tide, including meteorological effects.

title: Glossary
heading: HIGH WATER MARK
text: A reference mark on a structure or natural object, indicating the maximum stage of tide or flood.

title: Glossary
heading: HIGH WATER OF ORDINARY SPRING TIDES (HWOST)
text: A tidal datum appearing in some British publications, based on high water of ordinary spring tides.

title: Glossary
heading: HIGHER HIGH WATER (HHW)
text: The higher of the two high waters of any tidal day. The single high water occurring daily during periods when the tide is diurnal is considered to be a higher high water. (See Figure II-5-16.)

title: Glossary
heading: HIGHER LOW WATER (HLW)
text: The higher of two low waters of any tidal day. (See Figure II-5-16.)

title: Glossary
heading: HIGHEST ASTRONOMICAL TIDE (HAT)
text: The highest level of water which can be predicted to occur under any combination of astronomical conditions. This level may not be reached every year.

title: Glossary
heading: HINDCASTING
text: In wave prediction, the retrospective forecasting of waves using measured wind information.

title: Glossary
heading: HINTERLAND
text: The region lying inland from the coast. Also the inland area served by a port.

title: Glossary
heading: HISTORIC EVENT ANALYSIS
text: Extreme analysis based on hindcasting typically ten events over a period of 100 years.

title: Glossary
heading: HOLOCENE
text: An epoch of the QUATERNARY period, from the end of the PLEISTOCENE, about 8,000 years ago, to the present time. Syn: Recent.

title: Glossary
heading: HOMOPYCNAL FLOW
text: A condition in which the outflow jet from a river or coastal inlet and the water in the receiving basin are of the same density or are vertically mixed.

title: Glossary
heading: HOOK
text: A spit or narrow cape of sand or gravel which turns landward at the outer end; a RECURVED SPIT.

title: Glossary
heading: HOPPER DREDGE
text: Self-propelled floating plant which is capable of dredging material, storing it onboard, and transporting and placing the material at a specified disposal site. Often used to dredge inlets and then deposit the material along the open coast or offshore.

title: Glossary
heading: HORIZONTAL CLOSURE METHOD
text: Construction of a dam by dumping the materials from one or both banks, thus constricting the channel progressively laterally until the dam is closed. This method is also known as end dumping and point tipping

title: Glossary
heading: HURRICANE
text: An intense tropical cyclone in which winds tend to spiral inward toward a core of low pressure, with maximum surface wind velocities that equal or exceed 33.5 m/sec (75 mph or 65 knots) for several minutes or longer at some points. TROPICAL STORM is the term applied if maximum winds are less than 33.5 m/sec but greater than a whole gale (63 mph or 55 knots). Term is used in the Atlantic, Gulf of Mexico, and eastern Pacific.

title: Glossary
heading: HURRICANE PATH or TRACK
text: Line of movement (propagation) of the eye through an area.

title: Glossary
heading: HURRICANE STAGE HYDROGRAPH
text: A continuous graph representing water level stages that would be recorded in a gage well located at a specified point of interest during the passage of a particular hurricane, assuming that effects of relatively short-period waves are eliminated from the record by damping features of the gage well. Unless specifically excluded and separately accounted for, hurricane surge hydrographs are assumed to include effects of astronomical tides, barometric pressure differences, and all other factors that influence water level stages within a properly designed gage well located at a specified point.

title: Glossary
heading: HURRICANE WIND PATTERN or ISOVEL PATTERN
text: An actual or graphical representation of near-surface wind velocities covering the entire area of a hurricane at a particular instant. Isovels are lines connecting points of simultaneous equal wind velocities, usually referenced 9 meters (30 feet) above the surface, in meters per second, knots, or meters per hour; wind directions at various points are indicated by arrows or deflection angles on the isovel charts. Isovel charts are usually prepared at each hour during a hurricane, but for each half hour during critical periods.

title: Glossary
heading: HYDRAULIC DREDGE
text: Floating or (occasionally) truck-based plant which lifts the material through a suction pipe. It requires dilution water for material pickup, lift, and transportation. Often used to renourish beaches when material is pumped onto the shore from an offshore sand source.

title: Glossary
heading: HYDRAULIC RADIUS
text: Quotient of the wetted cross-sectional area and the wetted perimeter.

title: Glossary
heading: HYDRAULICALLY EQUIVALENT GRAINS
text: Sedimentary particles that settle at the same rate under the same conditions.

title: Glossary
heading: HYDROGRAPHY
text: (1) The description and study of seas, lakes, rivers and other waters. (2) The science of locating aids and dangers to navigation. (3) The description of physical properties of the waters of a region.

title: Glossary
heading: HYDROGRAPHIC PRESSURE
text: The pressure exerted by water at any given point in a body of water at rest.

title: Glossary
heading: HYPOPYCNAL FLOW
text: Outflow from a river or coastal inlet in which a wedge of less dense water flows over the denser sea water.

title: Glossary
heading: HYPOTHETICAL HURRICANE ("HYPOHURRICANE")
text: A representation of a hurricane, with specified characteristics, that is assumed to occur in a particular study area, following a specified path and timing sequence. TRANSPOSED--A hypohurricane based on the storm transposition principle, assumed to have wind patterns and other characteristics basically comparable to a specified hurricane of record, but transposed to follow a new path to serve as a basis for computing a hurricane surge hydrograph that would be expected at a selected point. Moderate adjustments in timing or rate of forward movement may also be made, if these are compatible with meteorological considerations and study objectives. HYPOHURRICANE BASED ON GENERALIZED PARAMETERS--Hypohurricane estimates based on various logical combinations of hurricane characteristics used in estimating hurricane surge magnitudes corresponding to a range of probabilities and potentialities. The STANDARD PROJECT HURRICANE is most commonly used for this purpose, but estimates corresponding to more severe or less severe assumptions are important in some project investigations. STANDARD PROJECT HURRICANE (SPH)--A hypothetical hurricane intended to represent the most severe combination of hurricane parameters that is reasonably characteristic of a specified region, excluding extremely rare combinations. It is further assumed that the SPH would approach a given project site from such direction, and at such rate of movement, to produce the highest HURRICANE SURGE HYDROGRAPH, considering pertinent hydraulic characteristics of the area. Based on this concept, and on extensive meteorological studies and probability analyses, a tabulation of "Standard Project Hurricane Index Characteristics" mutually agreed upon by representatives of the U. S. Weather Service and the Corps of Engineers,

title: Glossary
heading: ICE AGE
text: A loosely-used synonym of glacial epoch, or time of extensive glacial activity; specifically of the latest period of widespread continental glaciers, the PLEISTOCENE Epoch.

title: Glossary
heading: ICE FRONT
text: The floating vertical cliff forming the seaward edge of an ICE SHELF or other glacier that enters the sea.

title: Glossary
heading: ICE SHELF
text: A extensive sheet of ice which is attached to the land along one side but most of which is afloat and bounded on the seaward side by a steep cliff (ICE FRONT) rising 2 tp 50+ m above sea level. Common along polar coasts (Antarctica, Greenland), and generally of great breadth and sometimes extending tens or hundreds of km seaward from the continental coastline.

title: Glossary
heading: IMPERMEABLE GROIN
text: A GROIN constructed such that sand cannot pass through the structure (but sand may still move over or around it).

title: Glossary
heading: INCIDENT WAVE
text: Wave moving landward.

title: Glossary
heading: INDIAN SPRING LOW WATER
text: The approximate level of the mean of lower low waters at spring tides, used principally in the Indian Ocean and along the east coast of Asia. Also INDIAN TIDE PLANE.

title: Glossary
heading: INDIAN TIDE PLANE
text: The datum of INDIAN SPRING LOW WATER.

title: Glossary
heading: INFRAGRAVITY WAVE
text: Long waves with periods of 30 seconds to several minutes.

title: Glossary
heading: INLAND WATERS
text: Waters landward of the baseline (COASTLINE) from which the marginal seas are measured and over which complete sovereignty is exercised. Also known as "internal waters."

title: Glossary
heading: INLET
text: (1) A short, narrow waterway connecting a bay, lagoon, or similar body of water with a large parent body of water. (2) An arm of the sea (or other body of water) that is long compared to its width and may extend a considerable distance inland. See also TIDAL INLET.

title: Glossary
heading: INLET GORGE
text: Generally, the deepest region of an inlet channel.

title: Glossary
heading: INSHORE (ZONE)
text: In beach terminology, the zone of variable width extending from the LOW WATER LINE through the BREAKER ZONE. Also SHOREFACE. (See Figure A-1)

title: Glossary
heading: INSHORE CURRENT
text: Any current in or landward of the BREAKER ZONE.

title: Glossary
heading: INSULAR SHELF
text: The zone surrounding an island extending from the LOW WATER LINE to the depth (usually about 183 m; 100 fathoms) where there is a marked or rather steep descent toward the great depths.

title: Glossary
heading: INTERNAL WAVES
text: Waves that occur within a fluid whose density changes with depth, either abruptly at a sharp surface of discontinuity (an interface), or gradually. Their amplitude is greatest at the density discontinuity or, in the case of a gradual density change, somewhere in the interior of the fluid and not at the free upper surface where the surface waves have their maximum amplitude.

title: Glossary
heading: INTERTIDAL
text: The zone between the high and low water tides.

title: Glossary
heading: IRREGULAR WAVES
text: Waves with random wave periods (and in practice, also heights), which are typical for natural wind-induced waves.

title: Glossary
heading: IRROTATIONAL WAVE
text: A wave with fluid particles that do not revolve around an axis through their centers, although the particles themselves may travel in circular or nearly circular orbits. Irrotational waves may be PROGRESSIVE, STANDING, OSCILLATORY, or TRANSLATORY. For example, the Airy, Stokes, cnoidal, and solitary wave theories describe irrotational waves. Compare TROCHOIDAL WAVE.

title: Glossary
heading: ISOBATH
text: A contour line connecting points of equal water depths on a CHART.

title: Glossary
heading: ISOPACHYTE
text: Line connecting points on the seabed with an equal depth of sediment.

title: Glossary
heading: ISOVEL PATTERN
text: See HURRICANE WIND PATTERN.

title: Glossary
heading: ISTHMUS
text: A narrow strip of land, bordered on both sides by water, that connects two larger bodies of land. J

title: Glossary
heading: JET
text: To place (a pile, slab, or pipe) in the ground by means of a jet of water acting at the lower end.

title: Glossary
heading: JETTY
text: (1) (United States usage) On open seacoasts, a structure extending into a body of water, which is designed to prevent shoaling of a channel by littoral materials and to direct and confine the stream or tidal flow. Jetties are built at the mouths of rivers or tidal inlets to help deepen and stabilize a channel. (2) (British usage) WHARF or PIER. See TRAINING WALL.

title: Glossary
heading: JOINT PROBABILITY
text: The probability of two (or more) things occurring together.

title: Glossary
heading: JOINT PROBABILITY DENSITY
text: Function specifying the joint distribution of two (or more) variables.

title: Glossary
heading: JOINT RETURN PERIOD
text: Average period of time between occurrences of a given joint probability event.

title: Glossary
heading: JONSWAP SPECTRUM
text: Wave spectrum typical of growing deep water waves developed from field experiments and measurements of waves and wave spectra in the Joint North Sea Wave Peoject

title: Glossary
heading: KATABATIC WIND
text: Wind caused by cold air flowing down slopes due to gravitational acceleration.

title: Glossary
heading: KEY
text: A cay, esp. one of the low, insular banks of sand, coral, and limestone off the southern coast of Florida.

title: Glossary
heading: KINEMATIC VISCOSITY
text: The dynamic viscosity divided by the fluid density.

title: Glossary
heading: KINETIC ENERGY (OF WAVES)
text: In a progressive oscillatory wave, a summation of the energy of motion of the particles within the wave.

title: Glossary
heading: KNOLL
text: A submerged elevation of rounded shape rising less than 1000 meters from the ocean floor and of limited extent across the summit. Compare SEAMOUNT.

title: Glossary
heading: KNOT
text: The unit of speed used in navigation equal to 1 nautical mile (6,076.115 ft or 1,852 m) per hour. L

title: Glossary
heading: LAGGING OF TIDE
text: The periodic retardation in the time of occurrence of high and low water due to changes in the relative positions of the moon and sun.

title: Glossary
heading: LAGOON
text: A shallow body of water, like a pond or sound, partly or completely separated from the sea by a BARRIER ISLAND or REEF. Sometimes connected to the sea via an INLET. (See Figure IV-1-6.)

title: Glossary
heading: LAMINAR FLOW
text: Slow, smooth flow, with each drop of water traveling a smooth path parallel to its neighboring drops. Laminar flow is characteristic of low velocities, and particles of sediment in the flow zones are moved by rolling or SALTATION.

title: Glossary
heading: LAND BREEZE
text: A light wind blowing from the land to the sea, caused by unequal cooling of land and water masses.

title: Glossary
heading: LAND-SEA BREEZE
text: The combination of a land breeze and a sea breeze as a diurnal phenomenon.

title: Glossary
heading: LANDLOCKED
text: Enclosed, or nearly enclosed, by land--thus protected from the sea, as a bay or a harbor.

title: Glossary
heading: LANDMARK
text: A conspicuous object, natural or artificial, located near or on land, which aids in fixing the position of an observer.

title: Glossary
heading: LEAD LINE
text: A line, wire, or cord used in sounding (to obtain water depth). It is weighted at one end with a plummet (sounding lead). Also SOUNDING LINE.

title: Glossary
heading: LEDGE
text: A rocky formation forming a ridge or REEF, especially one underwater or near shore

title: Glossary
heading: LEE
text: (1) Shelter, or the part or side sheltered or turned away from the wind or waves. (2) (Chiefly nautical) The quarter or region toward which the wind blows.

title: Glossary
heading: LEEWARD
text: The direction toward which the wind is blowing; the direction toward which waves are traveling.

title: Glossary
heading: LENGTH OF WAVE
text: The horizontal distance between similar points on two successive waves measured perpendicularly to the crest.

title: Glossary
heading: LEVEE
text: (1) A ridge or EMBANKMENT of sand and silt, built up by a stream on its flood plain along both banks of its channel. (2) A large DIKE or artificial EMBANKMENT, often having an access road along the top, which is designed as part of a system to protect land from floods.

title: Glossary
heading: LIGHT BREEZE
text: A wind with velocity from 4 to 6 KNOTS.

title: Glossary
heading: LIMIT OF BACKRUSH (LIMIT OF BACKWASH)
text: See BACKRUSH, BACKWASH.

title: Glossary
heading: LITTORAL
text: Of or pertaining to a shore, especially of the sea. Often used as a general term for the coastal zone influenced by wave action, or, more specifically, the shore zone between the high and low water marks.

title: Glossary
heading: LITTORAL CELL
text: A reach of the coast that is isolated sedimentologically from adjacent coastal reaches and that features its own sources and sinks. Isolation is typically caused by protruding headlands, submarine canyons, inlets, and some river mouths that prevent littoral sediment from one cell to pass into the next. Cells may range in size from a multi-hundred meter POCKET BEACH in a rocky coast to a BARRIER ISLAND many tens of kilometers long.

title: Glossary
heading: LITTORAL CURRENT
text: See CURRENT, LITTORAL.

title: Glossary
heading: LITTORAL DEPOSITS
text: Deposits of littoral drift.

title: Glossary
heading: LITTORAL DRIFT, LITTORAL TRANSPORT
text: The movement of beach material in the littoral zone by waves and currents. Includes movement parallel (long shore drift) and sometimes also perpendicular (cross-shore transport) to the shore.

title: Glossary
heading: LITTORAL TRANSPORT RATE
text: Rate of transport of sedimentary material parallel or perpendicular to the shore in the littoral zone. Usually expressed in cubic meters (cubic yards) per year. Commonly synonymous with LONGSHORE TRANSPORT RATE.

title: Glossary
heading: LITTORAL ZONE
text: In beach terminology, an indefinite zone extending seaward from the shoreline to just beyond the breaker zone.

title: Glossary
heading: LOAD
text: The quantity of sediment transported by a current. It includes the suspended load of small particles and the BED LOAD of large particles that move along the bottom.

title: Glossary
heading: LONG WAVES
text: Waves with periods above about 30 seconds; can be generated by wave groups breaking in the surf zone. See also INFRAGRAVITY WAVES.

title: Glossary
heading: LONGSHORE
text: Parallel to and near the shoreline; ALONGSHORE.

title: Glossary
heading: LONGSHORE BAR
text: A sand ridge or ridges, running roughly parallel to the shoreline and extending along the shore outside the trough, that may be exposed at low tide or may occur below the water level in the offshore.

title: Glossary
heading: LONGSHORE CURRENT
text: See CURRENT, LONGSHORE.

title: Glossary
heading: LONGSHORE DRIFT
text: Movement of (beach) sediments approximately parallel to the coastline.

title: Glossary
heading: LONGSHORE TRANSPORT RATE
text: See LITTORAL TRANSPORT RATE.

title: Glossary
heading: LONGSHORE TROUGH
text: An elongate DEPRESSION or series of depressions extending along the lower BEACH or in the offshore zone inside the BREAKERS.

title: Glossary
heading: LOOP
text: That part of a STANDING WAVE where the vertical motion is greatest and the horizontal velocities are least. Loops (sometimes called ANTINODES) are associated with CLAPOTIS and with SEICHE action resulting from wave reflections. Compare NODE.

title: Glossary
heading: LOW TIDE (LOW WATER, LW)
text: The minimum elevation reached by each falling tide. See TIDE. (See Figure II-5-16.)

title: Glossary
heading: LOW TIDE TERRACE
text: A flat zone of the beach near the low water level.

title: Glossary
heading: LOW WATER (LW)
text: The minimum height reached by each falling tide. Nontechnically, also called LOW TIDE.

title: Glossary
heading: LOW WATER DATUM
text: An approximation to the plane of mean low water that has been adopted as a standard reference plane. See also DATUM, PLANE and CHART DATUM.

title: Glossary
heading: LOW WATER LINE
text: The line where the established LOW WATER DATUM intersects the shore. The plane of reference that constitutes the LOW WATER DATUM differs in different regions.

title: Glossary
heading: LOW WATER OF ORDINARY SPRING TIDES (LWOST)
text: A tidal datum appearing in some British publications, based on low water of ordinary spring tides.

title: Glossary
heading: LOWER HIGH WATER (LHW)
text: The lower of the two high waters of any tidal day. (See Figure II-5-16.)

title: Glossary
heading: LOWER LOW WATER DATUM
text: An approximation to the plane of MEAN LOWER LOW WATER that has been adopted as a standard reference plane for a limited area and is retained for an indefinite period regardless of the fact that it may differ slightly from a better determination of MEAN LOWER LOW WATER from a subsequent series of observations.

title: Glossary
heading: LOWER LOW WATER (LLW)
text: The lower of the two low waters of any tidal day. The single low water occurring daily during periods when the tide is diurnal is considered to be a lower low water. (See Figure II-5-16.)

title: Glossary
heading: LOWEST ASTRONOMICAL TIDE (LAT)
text: The lowest tide level which can be predicted to occur under average meteorological conditions and any combination of astronomical conditions. This level may not be reached every year.

title: Glossary
heading: LUNAR DAY
text: The time of rotation of the Earth with respect to the moon, or the interval between two successive upper transits of the moon over the meridian of a place. The mean lunar day is approximately 24.84 solar hours in length, or 1.035 times as great as the mean solar day. Also called TIDAL DAY.

title: Glossary
heading: LUNAR TIDE
text: The portion of the tide that can be attributed directly to attraction to the moon. M

title: Glossary
heading: MACH-STEM WAVE
text: Higher-than-normal wave generated when waves strike a structure at an oblique angle.

title: Glossary
heading: MACRO-TIDAL
text: Tidal range greater than 4 m.

title: Glossary
heading: MANAGED RETREAT
text: The deliberate setting back (moving landward) of the existing line of sea defense in order to obtain engineering or environmental advantages - also referred to as managed landward realignment. Sometimes refers to moving roads and utilities landward in the face of shore retreat.

title: Glossary
heading: MANGROVE
text: A tropical tree with interlacing prop roots, confined to low-lying brackish areas.

title: Glossary
heading: MARGIN, CONTINENTAL
text: A zone separating a continent from the deep-sea bottom.

title: Glossary
heading: MARGINAL PROBABILITY
text: The probability of a single variable in the context of a joint probability analysis.

title: Glossary
heading: MARGINAL RETURN PERIOD
text: The return period of a single variable in the context of a joint probability analysis.

title: Glossary
heading: MARGINAL SEA
text: The maritime belt over which a coastal state asserts sovereignty.

title: Glossary
heading: MARIGRAM
text: A graphic record of the rise and fall of the tide. The record is in the form of a curve in which time is represented by abscissas and the height of the tide by ordinates.

title: Glossary
heading: MARKER, REFERENCE
text: A mark of permanent character close to a survey station, to which it is related by an accurately measured distance and azimuth (or bearing).

title: Glossary
heading: MARKER, SURVEY
text: An object placed at the site of a station to identify the surveyed location of that station.

title: Glossary
heading: MARSH
text: (1) A tract of soft, wet land, usually vegetated by reeds, grasses and occasionally small shrubs. (2) Soft, wet area periodically or continuously flooded to a shallow depth, usually characterized by a particular subclass of grasses, cattails and other low plants.

title: Glossary
heading: MARSH, DIKED
text: A former salt marsh which has been protected by a DIKE.

title: Glossary
heading: MARSH, SALT
text: A marsh periodically flooded by salt water.

title: Glossary
heading: MASS TRANSPORT, SHOREWARD
text: The movement of water due to wave motion, which carries water through the BREAKER ZONE in the direction of wave propagation. Part of the NEARSHORE CURRENT SYSTEM.

title: Glossary
heading: MATTRESS
text: A blanket of brushwood or bamboo, poles, geotextile and reed lashed together to protect a shoreline, embankment or river/sea bed against erosion. Sometimes placed on the sea bed during JETTY construction to prevent stone from settling into soft bottom.

title: Glossary
heading: MEAN DEPTH
text: The average DEPTH of the water area between the still water level and the SHOREFACE profile from the waterline to any chosen distance seaward.

title: Glossary
heading: MEAN DIAMETER, GEOMETRIC
text: See GEOMETRIC MEAN DIAMETER.

title: Glossary
heading: MEAN HIGH WATER SPRINGS (MHWS)
text: The average height of the high water occurring at the time of spring tides.

title: Glossary
heading: MEAN HIGH WATER (MHW)
text: The average height of the high waters over a 19-year period. For shorter periods of observations, corrections are applied to eliminate known variations and reduce the results to the equivalent of a mean 19-year value. All high water heights are included in the average where the type of tide is either semidiurnal or mixed. Only the higher high water heights are included in the average where the type of tide is diurnal. So determined, mean high water in the latter case is the same as mean higher high water.

title: Glossary
heading: MEAN HIGHER HIGH WATER (MHHW)
text: The average height of the higher high waters over a 19-year period. For shorter periods of observation, corrections are applied to eliminate known variations and reduce the result to the equivalent of a mean 19-year value.

title: Glossary
heading: MEAN LOW WATER (MLW)
text: The average height of the low waters over a 19-year period. For shorter periods of observations, corrections are applied to eliminate known variations and reduce the results to the equivalent of a mean 19-year value. All low water heights are included in the average where the type of tide is either semidiurnal or mixed. Only lower low water heights are included in the average where the type of tide is diurnal. So determined, mean low water in the latter case is the same as mean lower low water.

title: Glossary
heading: MEAN LOW WATER SPRINGS
text: The average height of low waters occurring at the time of the spring tides. It is usually derived by taking a plane depressed below the half-tide level by an amount equal to one-half the spring range of tide, necessary corrections being applied to reduce the result to a mean value. This plane is used to a considerable extent for hydrographic work outside of the United States and is the plane of reference for the Pacific approaches to the Panama Canal. Frequently abbreviated to LOW WATER SPRINGS.

title: Glossary
heading: MEAN LOWER LOW WATER (MLLW)
text: The average height of the lower low waters over a 19-year period. For shorter periods of observations, corrections are applied to eliminate known variations and reduce the results to the equivalent of a mean 19-year value. Frequently abbreviated to LOWER LOW WATER.

title: Glossary
heading: MEAN RANGE OF TIDE
text: The difference in height between MEAN HIGH WATER and MEAN LOW WATER.

title: Glossary
heading: MEAN RISE OF THE TIDE
text: The height of MEAN HIGH WATER above the plane of reference or DATUM of chart.

title: Glossary
heading: MEAN SEA LEVEL
text: The average height of the surface of the sea for all stages of the tide over a 19-year period, usually determined from hourly height readings. Not necessarily equal to MEAN TIDE LEVEL. It is also the average water level that would exist in the absence of tides.

title: Glossary
heading: MEAN STEEPNESS
text: The ratio of the MEAN DEPTH to the horizontal distance over which the MEAN DEPTH was determined.

title: Glossary
heading: MEAN TIDE LEVEL
text: A plane midway between MEAN HIGH WATER and MEAN LOW WATER. Not necessarily equal to MEAN SEA LEVEL. Also HALF-TIDE LEVEL.

title: Glossary
heading: MEAN WATER LEVEL
text: The mean surface level as determined by averaging the heights of the water at equal intervals of time, usually at hourly intervals.

title: Glossary
heading: MEAN WAVE HEIGHT
text: The mean of all individual waves in an observation interval of approximately half an hour. In case of a Rayleighdistribution 63% of the significant wave height.

title: Glossary
heading: MEANDERING
text: A single channel having a pattern of successive deviations in alignment which results in a more or less sinusoidal course.

title: Glossary
heading: MECHANICAL DREDGE
text: Mechanical plant characterized by the use of some form of bucket to excavate and raise the bottom material Normally, the mechanical dredge deposits material into a barge that transports it to the disposal site, but in some cases, the dredged material can be deposited directly in-water or placed on the bank immediately adjacent to the work area. Mechanical dredges may be classified into two sub-groups by how their buckets are connected to the dredge: (1) wire rope (i.e., clamshell or dragline), and (2) structurally (i.e., a backhoe).

title: Glossary
heading: MEDIAN DIAMETER
text: The diameter which marks the division of a given sand sample into two equal parts by weight, one part containing all grains larger than that diameter and the other part containing all grains smaller.

title: Glossary
heading: MEGARIPPLE
text: See SAND WAVE.

title: Glossary
heading: MESO-TIDAL
text: Tidal range between 2 m and 4 m.

title: Glossary
heading: METEOROLOGICAL TIDES
text: Tidal constituents having their origin in the daily or seasonal variation in weather conditions which may occur with some degree of periodicity.

title: Glossary
heading: MICRO-TIDAL
text: Tidal range less than 2 m.

title: Glossary
heading: MID-EXTREME TIDE
text: A plane midway between the extreme high water and the extreme LOW WATER occurring in any locality.

title: Glossary
heading: MIDDLE-GROUND SHOAL
text: A shoal formed by ebb and flood tides in the middle of the channel of the LAGOON or estuary end of an inlet.

title: Glossary
heading: MINERAL
text: A naturally occurring, inorganic, crystalline solid that has a definite chemical composition and possesses characteristic physical properties.

title: Glossary
heading: MINIMUM DURATION
text: See DURATION, MINIMUM.

title: Glossary
heading: MINIMUM FETCH
text: The least distance in which steady-state wave conditions will develop for a wind of given speed blowing a given duration of time.

title: Glossary
heading: MIST
text: Water vapor suspended in the air in very small drops finer than rain, larger than fog.

title: Glossary
heading: MIXED CURRENT
text: Type of tidal current characterized by a conspicious velocity difference between the two floods or two ebbs usually occurring each tidal day.

title: Glossary
heading: MIXED TIDE
text: A type of tide in which the presence of a diurnal wave is conspicuous by a large inequality in either the high or low water heights, with two high waters and two low waters usually occurring each tidal day. In strictness, all tides are mixed, but the name is usually applied without definite limits to the tide intermediate to those predominantly semidiurnal and those predominantly diurnal. (See Figure II-5-16.)

title: Glossary
heading: MOLE
text: In coastal terminology, a massive land-connected, solid-fill structure of earth (generally revetted), masonry, or large stone, which may serve as a breakwater or pier.

title: Glossary
heading: MONOCHROMATIC WAVES
text: A series of waves generated in a laboratory, each of which has the same length and period.

title: Glossary
heading: MONOLITHIC
text: Like a single stone or block. In coastal structures, the type of construction in which the structure's component parts are bound together to act as one.

title: Glossary
heading: MORAINE
text: An accumulation of earth, stones, etc., deposited by a glacier, usually in the form of a mound, ridge or other prominence on the terrain.

title: Glossary
heading: MORPHODYNAMICS
text: (1) The mutual interaction and adjustment of the seafloor topography and fluid dynamics involving the motion of sediment. (2) The coupled suite of mutually interdependent hydrodynamic processes, seafloor morphologies, and sequences of change.

title: Glossary
heading: MORPHOLOGICALLY AVERAGED
text: Single wave condition producing the same net longshore drift as a given proportion of the annual wave climate.

title: Glossary
heading: MORPHOLOGY
text: River/estuary/lake/seabed form and its change with time.

title: Glossary
heading: MOUTH
text: Entrance to an inland water body (e.g., river).

title: Glossary
heading: MUD
text: A fluid-to-plastic mixture of finely divided particles of solid material and water.

title: Glossary
heading: MUD FLAT
text: A level area of fine silt and clay along a shore alternately covered or uncovered by the tide or covered by shallow water. N

title: Glossary
heading: NATIONAL TIDAL DATUM EPOCH (NTDE)
text: A period of 19 years adopted by the National Ocean Service as the period over which observations of tides are to be taken and reduced to average values for tidal datums.

title: Glossary
heading: NATURAL TRACER
text: A component of a sediment deposit that is unique to a particular source and can be used to identify the source and transport routes to a place of deposition.

title: Glossary
heading: NAUTICAL MILE
text: The length of a minute of arc, 1/21,600 of an average great circle of the Earth. Generally one minute of latitude is considered equal to one nautical mile. The accepted United States value as of 1 July 1959 is 1,852 meters (6,076.115 feet), approximately 1.15 times as long as the U.S. statute mile of 5,280 feet. Also GEOGRAPHICAL MILE.

title: Glossary
heading: NAVIGABLE WATERS
text: Waters that are either tidally-influenced or navigable in fact.

title: Glossary
heading: NAVIGATION PROJECT
text: A specific and fixed geographic area within a water body or series of water bodies that has been authorized for navigation by some group or organization (often a national government).

title: Glossary
heading: NEAP HIGH WATER
text: See NEAP TIDE.

title: Glossary
heading: NEAP LOW WATER
text: See NEAP TIDE.

title: Glossary
heading: NEAP RANGE
text: See NEAP TIDE.

title: Glossary
heading: NEAP TIDAL CURRENT
text: Tidal current of decreased velocity occurring semimonthly as the result of the moon being in quadrature.

title: Glossary
heading: NEAP TIDE
text: Tide of decreased range occurring semimonthly as the result of the moon being in quadrature. The NEAP RANGE of the tide is the average semidiurnal range occurring at the time of neap tides and is most conveniently computed from the harmonic constants. The NEAP RANGE is typically 10 to 30 percent smaller than the mean range where the type of tide is either semidiurnal or mixed and is of no practical significance where the type of tide is DIURNAL. The average height of the high waters of the neap tide is called NEAP HIGH WATER or HIGH WATER NEAPS (MHWN), and the average height of the corresponding LOW WATER is called NEAP LOW WATER or LOW WATER NEAPS (MLWN).

title: Glossary
heading: NEARSHORE
text: (1) In beach terminology an indefinite zone extending seaward from the SHORELINE well beyond the BREAKER ZONE. (2) The zone which extends from the swash zone to the position marking the start of the offshore zone, typically at water depths of the order of 20 m.

title: Glossary
heading: NEARSHORE BERM
text: Artificial berm built in shallow water using dredged material. Often, the berm is intended to renourish the adjacent and downdrift shore over time under the influence of waves and currents.

title: Glossary
heading: NEARSHORE CIRCULATION
text: The ocean circulation pattern composed of the NEARSHORE CURRENTS and the COASTAL CURRENTS.

title: Glossary
heading: NEARSHORE CURRENT SYSTEM
text: The current system caused primarily by wave action in and near the breaker zone, and which consists of four parts: the shoreward mass transport of water; longshore currents; seaward return flow, including rip currents; and the longshore movement of the expanding heads of rip currents. See also NEARSHORE CIRCULATION.

title: Glossary
heading: NECK
text: (1) The narrow strip of land which connects a peninsula with the mainland, or connects two ridges. (2) The narrow band (rip) of water flowing seaward through the surf. See also RIP CURRENT.

title: Glossary
heading: NESS
text: Roughly triangular promontory of land jutting into the sea, often consisting of mobile material, i.e. a beach form.

title: Glossary
heading: NETWORK
text: A set consisting of: (a) stations for which geometric relationships have been determined and which are so related that removal of one station from the set will affect the relationships (distances, directions, coordinates, etc.) between the other stations; and (b) lines connecting the stations to show this interdependence.

title: Glossary
heading: NIP
text: The cut made by waves in a shoreline of emergence.

title: Glossary
heading: NODAL ZONE
text: An area in which the predominant direction of the LONGSHORE TRANSPORT changes.

title: Glossary
heading: NODE
text: That part of a STANDING WAVE where the vertical motion is least and the horizontal velocities are greatest. Nodes are associated with CLAPOTIS and with SEICHE action resulting from wave reflections. Compare LOOP.

title: Glossary
heading: NOURISHMENT
text: The process of replenishing a beach. It may occur naturally by longshore transport, or be brought about artificially by the deposition of dredged materials or of materials trucked in from upland sites.

title: Glossary
heading: NUMERICAL MODELING
text: Refers to analysis of coastal processes using computational models. O

title: Glossary
heading: OCEANOGRAPHY
text: The study of the sea, embracing and indicating all knowledge pertaining to the sea's physical boundaries, the chemistry and physics of seawater, marine biology, and marine geology.

title: Glossary
heading: OFFSHORE
text: (1) In beach terminology, the comparatively flat zone of variable width, extending from the SHOREFACE to the edge of the CONTINENTAL SHELF. It is continually submerged. (2) The direction seaward from the shore. (3) The zone beyond the nearshore zone where sediment motion induced by waves alone effectively ceases and where the influence of the sea bed on wave action is small in comparison with the effect of wind. (4) The breaker zone directly seaward of the low tide line. (See Figure A-1)

title: Glossary
heading: OFFSHORE BARRIER
text: See BARRIER BEACH.

title: Glossary
heading: OFFSHORE BREAKWATER
text: A BREAKWATER built towards the seaward limit of the littoral zone, parallel (or nearly parallel) to the shore.

title: Glossary
heading: OFFSHORE CURRENT
text: (1) Any current in the offshore zone. (2) Any current flowing away from shore.

title: Glossary
heading: OFFSHORE WIND
text: A wind blowing seaward from the land in the coastal area.

title: Glossary
heading: ONSHORE
text: A direction landward from the sea.

title: Glossary
heading: ONSHORE WIND
text: A wind blowing landward from the sea in the coastal area.

title: Glossary
heading: OPPOSING WIND
text: In wave forecasting, a wind blowing in a direction opposite to the ocean-wave advance; generally, a headwind.

title: Glossary
heading: ORBIT
text: In water waves, the path of a water particle affected by the wave motion. In deepwater waves the orbit is nearly circular, and in shallow-water waves the orbit is nearly elliptical. In general, the orbits are slightly open in the direction of wave motion, giving rise to MASS TRANSPORT. (See Figure II-1-4.)

title: Glossary
heading: ORBITAL CURRENT
text: The flow of water accompanying the orbital movement of the water particles in a wave. Not to be confused with wave-generated LITTORAL CURRENTS. (See Figure II-1-4.)

title: Glossary
heading: ORDINARY HIGH WATER MARK (OHWM)
text: That mark that will be found by examining the bed and banks and ascertaining where the presence and action of waters are so common and usual, and so long continued in all ordinary years, as to mark upon the soil a character distinct from that of the abutting upland, in respect to vegetation as that condition exists on June 1, 1971, as it may naturally change thereafter, or as it may change thereafter in accordance with permits issued by a local government. Also defined as MEAN HIGH WATER LINE (Shalowitz 1962).

title: Glossary
heading: ORDINARY TIDE
text: This expression is not used in a technical sense by the U.S. Coast and Geodetic Survey, but the word "ordinary" when applied to tides, may be taken as the equivalent of the word "mean". Thus "ordinary HIGH WATER LINE" may be assumed to be the same as "mean high water line".

title: Glossary
heading: ORTHOGONAL
text: On a wave-refraction diagram, a line drawn perpendicularly to the wave crests. Also called WAVE RAY. (See Figure II-3-4)

title: Glossary
heading: OSCILLATION
text: (1) A periodic motion backward and forward. (2) Vibration or variance above and below a mean value.

title: Glossary
heading: OSCILLATORY WAVE
text: A wave in which each individual particle oscillates about a point with little or no permanent change in mean position. The term is commonly applied to progressive oscillatory waves in which only the form advances, the individual particles moving in closed or nearly closed orbits. Compare WAVE OF TRANSLATION. See also ORBIT.

title: Glossary
heading: OUTCROP
text: A surface exposure of bare rock, not covered by soil or vegetation.

title: Glossary
heading: OUTFALL
text: A structure extending into a body of water for the purpose of discharging sewage, storm runoff, or cooling water.

title: Glossary
heading: OUTFLANKING
text: EROSION behind or around the land-based end of a GROIN, JETTY, or BREAKWATER or the terminus of a BULKHEAD, REVETMENT, or SEAWALL, usually causing failure of the structure or its function

title: Glossary
heading: OVERSPLASH
text: The water that splashes over the top of a BREAKWATER, SEAWALL, etc.

title: Glossary
heading: OVERTOPPING
text: Passing of water over the top of a structure as a result of wave runup or surge action.

title: Glossary
heading: OVERWASH
text: (1) The part of the UPRUSH that runs over the crest of a BERM or structure and does not flow directly back to the ocean or lake. (2) The effect of waves overtopping a COASTAL DEFENSE, often carrying sediment landwards which is then lost to the beach system. P

title: Glossary
heading: PARAPET
text: A low wall built along the edge of a structure such as a SEAWALL or QUAY.

title: Glossary
heading: PARTICLE VELOCITY
text: The velocity induced by wave motion with which a specific water particle moves within a wave.

title: Glossary
heading: PATCH REEF
text: A moundlike or flat-topped organic REEF, generally less than 1 km across, frequently forming part of a larger reef complex.

title: Glossary
heading: PASS
text: In hydrographic usage, a navigable channel through a bar, REEF, or shoal, or between closely adjacent islands. On the Gulf of Mexico coast, inlets are often known as passes (e.g., Sabine Pass).

title: Glossary
heading: PEAK PERIOD
text: The wave period determined by the inverse of the frequency at which the wave energy spectrum reaches its maximum.

title: Glossary
heading: PEBBLES
text: Beach material usually well-rounded and between about 4 mm to 64 mm diameter. See SOIL CLASSIFICATION.

title: Glossary
heading: PENINSULA
text: An elongated body of land nearly surrounded by water and connected to a larger body of land by a neck or isthmus.

title: Glossary
heading: PERCHED BEACH
text: A beach or fillet of sand retained above the otherwise normal profile level by a submerged dike.

title: Glossary
heading: PERCOLATION
text: The process by which water flows through the interstices of a sediment. Specifically, in wave phenomena, the process by which wave action forces water through the interstices of the bottom sediment and which tends to reduce wave heights.

title: Glossary
heading: PERIGEAN RANGE
text: The average semidiurnal range occurring at the time of the PERIGEAN TIDES and most conveniently computed from the harmonic constants. It is larger than the mean range where the type of tide is either semidiurnal or mixed and is of no practical significance where the type of tide is diurnal.

title: Glossary
heading: PERIGEAN TIDAL CURRENTS
text: Tidal currents of increased velocity occurring monthly as the result of the moon being in perigee (i.e., at the point in its orbit nearest the Earth).

title: Glossary
heading: PERIGEAN TIDES
text: Tides of increased range occurring monthly as the result of the moon being in perigee.

title: Glossary
heading: PERIODIC CURRENT
text: A current caused by the tide-producing forces of the moon and the sun; a part of the same general movement of the sea that is manifested in the vertical rise and fall of the tides. See also CURRENT, FLOOD and CURRENT, EBB.

title: Glossary
heading: PERMANENT CURRENT
text: A current that runs continuously, independent of the tides and temporary causes. Permanent currents include the freshwater discharge of a river and the currents that form the general circulatory systems of the oceans.

title: Glossary
heading: PERMEABILITY
text: The property of bulk material (sand, crushed rock, soft rock in situ) which permit movement of water through its pores.

title: Glossary
heading: PERMEABLE GROIN
text: A GROIN with openings or voids large enough to permit passage of appreciable quantities of LITTORAL DRIFT through the structure.

title: Glossary
heading: PETROGRAPHY
text: The systematic description and classification of rocks.

title: Glossary
heading: PETROLOGY
text: That branch of geology which treats the scientific study of rocks.

title: Glossary
heading: PHASE
text: In surface wave motion, a point in the period to which the wave motion has advanced with respect to a given initial reference point.

title: Glossary
heading: PHASE INEQUALITY
text: Variations in the tides or tidal currents associated with changes in the phase of the Moon in relation to the Sun.

title: Glossary
heading: PHASE VELOCITY
text: Propagation velocity of an individual wave as opposed to the velocity of a wave group.

title: Glossary
heading: PHI GRADE SCALE
text: A logarithmic transformation of the Wentworth grade scale for size classifications of sediment grains based on the negative logarithm to the base 2 of the particle diameter: = $-\log {2}d.$ See SOIL CLASSIFICATION.

title: Glossary
heading: PHOTIC ZONE
text: The zone extending downward from the ocean surface within which the light is sufficient to sustain photosynthesis. The depth of this layer varies with water clarity, time of year and cloud cover, but is about 100 m in the open ocean. It may be considered the Depth to which all light is filtered out except for about one percent and may be calculated as about two and one-half times the depth of a SECCHI DISK reading.

title: Glossary
heading: PHOTOGRAMMETRY
text: The science of deducing the physical dimensions of objects from measurements on images (usually photographs) of the objects.

title: Glossary
heading: PHOTOMOSAIC
text: An assemblage of photographs, each of which shows part of a region, put together in such a way that each point in the region appears once and only once in the assemblage, and scale variation is minimized.

title: Glossary
heading: PHREATIC LEVEL
text: Upper surface of an unconfined aquifer (e.g. the top sand layer in a dike) at which the pressure in the groundwater is equal to atmospheric pressure.

title: Glossary
heading: PHYSICAL GEOLOGY
text: A large division of Geology concerned with earth materials, changes of the surface and interior of the earth, and the forces that cause those changes.

title: Glossary
heading: PHYSICAL MODELING
text: Refers to the investigation of coastal or riverine processes using a scaled model.

title: Glossary
heading: PIER
text: A structure, usually of open construction, extending out into the water from the shore, to serve as a landing place, recreational facility, etc., rather than to afford coastal protection or affect the movement of water. In the Great Lakes, a term sometimes improperly applied to jetties.

title: Glossary
heading: PIERSON-MOSKOWITZ SPECTRUM
text: Wave spectrum typical of fully-developed deep water waves.

title: Glossary
heading: PIEZOMETRIC SURFACE
text: The level at which the hydrostatic water pressure in an aquifer will stand if it is free to seek equilibrium with the atmosphere. For artesian wells, this is above the ground surface.

title: Glossary
heading: PILE
text: A long, heavy timber or section of concrete or metal that is driven or jetted into the earth or seabed to serve as a support or protection.

title: Glossary
heading: PILING
text: A group of piles.

title: Glossary
heading: PIPING
text: Erosion of closed flow channels (tunnels) by the passage of water through soil; flow underneath structures, carrying away particles, may endanger the stability of the structure.

title: Glossary
heading: PLACER DEPOSITS
text: Mineral deposits consisting of dense, resistant and often economically valuable minerals which have been weathered from TERRIGENOUS rocks, transported to the sea and concentrated in marine sediments by wave or current action.

title: Glossary
heading: PLACER MINE
text: Surface mines in which valuable mineral grains are extracted from stream bar or beach deposits.

title: Glossary
heading: PLAIN, COASTAL
text: See COASTAL PLAIN.

title: Glossary
heading: PLANFORM
text: The outline or shape of a body of water as determined by the still-water line.

title: Glossary
heading: PLATEAU
text: A land area (usually extensive) having a relatively level surface raised sharply above adjacent land on at least one side; table land. A similar undersea feature.

title: Glossary
heading: PLEISTOCENE
text: An epoch of the Quaternary Period characterized by several glacial ages.

title: Glossary
heading: PLUNGE POINT
text: (1) For a plunging wave, the point at which the wave curls over and falls. (2) The final breaking point of the waves just before they rush up on the beach. (See Figure A-1)

title: Glossary
heading: PLUNGING BREAKER
text: See BREAKER.

title: Glossary
heading: POCKET BEACH
text: A beach, usually small, in a coastal reentrant or between two littoral barriers (often rocky headlands).

title: Glossary
heading: POINT
text: (1) The extreme end of a CAPE, or the outer end of any land area protruding into the water, usually less prominent than a CAPE. (2) A low profile shoreline promontory of more or less triangular shape, the top of which extends seaward.

title: Glossary
heading: POORLY-SORTED (POORLY-GRADED)
text: Said of a clastic sediment or rock that consists of particles of many sizes mixed together in an unsystematic manner so that no one size class predominates.

title: Glossary
heading: PORE PRESSURE
text: The interstitial pressure of water within a mass of soil or rock.

title: Glossary
heading: POROSITY
text: Percentage of the total volume of a soil sample not occupied by solid particles but by air and water, η = $V {v}/V {T}$ × 100.

title: Glossary
heading: PORT
text: A place where vessels may discharge or receive cargo; it may be the entire harbor including its approaches and anchorages, or only the commercial part of a harbor where the QUAYS, WHARVES, facilities for transfer of cargo, docks, and repair shops are situated. Protection may be provided by natural or artificial features.

title: Glossary
heading: POTENTIAL ENERGY OF WAVES
text: In a progressive oscillatory wave, the energy resulting from the elevation or depression of the water surface from the undisturbed level.

title: Glossary
heading: PRISM
text: See TIDAL PRISM.

title: Glossary
heading: PROBABILITY
text: The chance that a prescribed event will occur, represented by a number (p) in the range 0 - 1. It can be estimated empirically from the relative frequency (i.e. the number of times the particular event occurs, divided by the total count of all events in the class considered).

title: Glossary
heading: PROBABILITY DENSITY
text: Function specifying the distribution of a variable.

title: Glossary
heading: PROBABLE MAXIMUM WATER LEVEL
text: A hypothetical water level (exclusive of wave runup from normal wind-generated waves) that might result from the most severe combination of hydrometeorological, geoseismic, and other geophysical factors and that is considered reasonably possible in the region involved, with each of these factors considered as affecting the locality in a maximum manner. This level represents the physical response of a body of water to maximum applied phenomena such as hurricanes, moving squall lines, other cyclonic meteorological events, tsunamis, and astronomical tide combined with maximum probable ambient hydrological conditions such as wave setup, rainfall, runoff, and river flow. It is a water level with virtually no risk of being exceeded.

title: Glossary
heading: PRODELTA
text: The part of a DELTA that is below the effective depth of wave erosion, lying beyond the delta front and sloping down into the basin into which the delta is advancing.

title: Glossary
heading: PROFILE, BEACH
text: The intersection of the ground surface with a vertical plane; typically perpendicular to the local shoreline, and may extend from the behind the DUNE line or the top of a bluff to well seaward of the breaker zone. (See Figure A-1)

title: Glossary
heading: PROGRESSION (of a beach)
text: See ADVANCE.

title: Glossary
heading: PROGRESSIVE WAVE
text: A wave that moves relative to a fixed coordinate system in a fluid. The direction in which it moves is termed the direction of wave propagation.

title: Glossary
heading: PROMONTORY
text: A high point of land projecting into a body of water; a HEADLAND.

title: Glossary
heading: PROPAGATION OF WAVES
text: The transmission of waves through water.

title: Glossary
heading: PROTOTYPE
text: In laboratory usage, the full-scale structure, concept, or phenomenon used as a basis for constructing a scale model or copy. Q

title: Glossary
heading: QUARRY RUN
text: Waste of generally small material, in a quarry, left after selection of larger grading.

title: Glossary
heading: QUARRYSTONE
text: Any stone processed from a quarry.

title: Glossary
heading: QUATERNARY
text: (1) The youngest geologic period; includes the present time. (2) The latest period of time in the stratigraphic column, 0 – 2 million years, represented by local accumulations of glacial (PLEISTOCENE) and post-glacial (HOLOCENE) deposits which continue, without change of fauna, from the top of the Pliocene (Tertiary). The quaternary appears to be an artificial division of time to separate pre-human from post-human sedimentation. As thus defined, the quaternary is increasing in duration as man's ancestry becomes better understood.

title: Glossary
heading: QUAY (pronounced KEY)
text: A stretch of paved bank, or a solid artificial landing place parallel to the navigable waterway, for use in loading and unloading vessels.

title: Glossary
heading: QUICKSAND
text: Loose, yielding, wet sand which offers no support to heavy objects. The upward flow of the water has a velocity that eliminates contact pressures between the sand grains and causes the sand-water mass to behave like a fluid that yields easily to pressure and tends to suck down heavy objects. R

title: Glossary
heading: RADAR
text: An instrument for determining the distance and direction to an object by measuring the time needed for radio signals to travel from the instrument to the object and back, and by measuring the angle through which the instrument's antenna has traveled.

title: Glossary
heading: RADIOACTIVE DATING (RADIOMETRIC DATING)
text: Calculating an age in years for geologic materials by measuring the presence of a short-life radioactive element (e.g., carbon-14) or a long-life element (e.g., potassium-40/argon-40). The term applies to all methods of age determination based on nuclear decay of naturally-occurring radioactive isotopes. Carbon-14 methods are often used to determine the age of peat or wood found in BARRIER ISLANDS.

title: Glossary
heading: RADIUS OF MAXIMUM WINDS
text: Distance from the eye of a hurricane, where surface and wind velocities are zero, to the place where surface windspeeds are maximum.

title: Glossary
heading: RAISED BEACH
text: A wave-cut platform, with or without a covering of beach materials, which is now raised above the present sealevel.

title: Glossary
heading: RANDOM WAVES
text: The laboratory simulation of irregular sea states that occur in nature.

title: Glossary
heading: RANGE OF TIDE
text: The difference in height between consecutive high and low waters. The MEAN RANGE is the difference between MEAN HIGH WATER and MEAN LOW WATER. The GREAT DIURNAL RANGE or DIURNAL RANGE is the difference in height between MEAN HIGHER HIGH WATER (MHHW) and MEAN LOWER LOW WATER (MLLW). Where the type of tide is diurnal, the mean range is the same as the diurnal range.

title: Glossary
heading: RAY, WAVE
text: See ORTHOGONAL.

title: Glossary
heading: RAYLEIGH DISTRIBUTION
text: A model probability distribution, commonly used in analysis of waves.

title: Glossary
heading: REACH
text: (1) An arm of the ocean extending into the land, e.g., an ESTUARY. (2) A straight section of restricted waterway that is uniform with respect to discharge, slope, and cross-section.

title: Glossary
heading: RECENT
text: (Geological) A synonym of HOLOCENE. See also QUATERNARY.

title: Glossary
heading: RECESSION
text: (1) A continuing landward movement of the shoreline. (2) A net landward movement of the shoreline over a specified time.

title: Glossary
heading: RECHARGE
text: The addition of new water to an AQUIFER or to the zone of saturation.

title: Glossary
heading: RECTIFICATION
text: The process of producing, from a tilted or oblique photograph, a photograph from which displacement caused by tilt has been removed.

title: Glossary
heading: RECURVED SPIT
text: A spit whose outer end in turned landward by current deflection, by the opposing action of two or more currents, or by WAVE REFRACTION; a HOOK.

title: Glossary
heading: RED TIDE
text: Discoloration of surface waters, most frequently in COASTAL ZONES, caused by large concentrations of microorganisms.

title: Glossary
heading: REEF
text: An offshore consolidated rock hazard to navigation, with a least depth of about 20 meters (10 fathoms) or less. Often refers to coral FRINGING REEFS in tropical waters.

title: Glossary
heading: REEF, ATOLL
text: See ATOLL.

title: Glossary
heading: REEF, BARRIER
text: See BARRIER REEF.

title: Glossary
heading: REEF BREAKWATER
text: Rubble mound of single-sized stones with a crest at or below sea level which is allowed to be (re)shaped by the waves.

title: Glossary
heading: REEF, FRINGING
text: See FRINGING REEF.

title: Glossary
heading: REFERENCE PLANE
text: The plane to which sounding and tidal data are referred. See DATUM PLANE.

title: Glossary
heading: REFERENCE POINT
text: (1) A specified location (in plan elevation) to which measurements are referred. (2) In beach material studies, a specified point within the REFERENCE ZONE.

title: Glossary
heading: REFERENCE STATION
text: A place for which tidal constants have previously been determined and which is used as a standard for the comparison of simultaneous observations at a second station. Also, a station for which independent daily predictions are given in the tide or current tables from which corresponding predictions are obtained for other stations by means of differences or factors.

title: Glossary
heading: REFERENCE ZONE
text: In regard to beach measuring procedure, the part of the FORESHORE subject to wave action (between the Limit of UPRUSH and the Limit of BACKWASH) at mid-tide stage. In areas of great tidal range a more complex definition is needed.

title: Glossary
heading: REFLECTED WAVE
text: That part of an incident wave that is returned seaward when a wave impinges on a steep beach, barrier, or other reflecting surface.

title: Glossary
heading: REFLECTION
text: The process by which the energy of the wave is returned seaward.

title: Glossary
heading: REFRACTION (of water waves)
text: (1) The process by which the direction of a wave moving in shallow water at an angle to the contours is changed: the part of the wave advancing in shallower water moves more slowly than that part still advancing in deeper water, causing the wave crest to bend toward alinement with the underwater contours. (2) The bending of wave crests by currents. (See Figure II-6-11.)

title: Glossary
heading: REFRACTION COEFFICIENT
text: The square root of the ratio of the distance between adjacent orthogonals in deep water to their distance apart in shallow water at a selected point. When multiplied by the SHOALING FACTOR and a factor for friction and percolation, this becomes the WAVE HEIGHT COEFFICIENT or the ratio of the refracted wave height at any point to the deepwater wave height. Also, the square root of the ENERGY COEFFICIENT.

title: Glossary
heading: REFRACTION DIAGRAM
text: A drawing showing positions of wave crests and/or orthogonals in a given area for a specific deepwater wave period and direction. (See Figure II-6-11.)

title: Glossary
heading: REGULAR WAVES
text: Waves with a single height, period, and direction.

title: Glossary
heading: RESERVOIR
text: An artificial lake, basin or tank in which a large quantity of water can be stored.

title: Glossary
heading: RESIDUAL (WATER LEVEL)
text: The components of water level not attributable to astronomical effects.

title: Glossary
heading: RESONANCE
text: The phenomenon of amplification of a free wave or oscillation of a system by a forced wave or oscillation of exactly equal period. The forced wave may arise from an impressed force upon the system or from a boundary condition.

title: Glossary
heading: RETARDATION
text: The amount of time by which corresponding tidal phases grow later day by day (about 50 minutes).

title: Glossary
heading: RETROGRESSION (of a beach)
text: See RECESSION.

title: Glossary
heading: RETURN PERIOD
text: Average period of time between occurrences of a given event.

title: Glossary
heading: REVERSING TIDAL CURRENT
text: A tidal current that flows alternately in approximately opposite directions with a SLACK WATER at each reversal of direction. Currents of this type usually occur in rivers and straits where the direction of flow is more or less restricted to certain channels. When the movement is towards the shore, the current is said to be flooding, and when in the opposite direction it is said to be ebbing.

title: Glossary
heading: REVETMENT
text: (1) A facing of stone, concrete, etc., to protect an EMBANKMENT, or shore structure, against erosion by wave action or currents. (2) A retaining wall. (3) Facing of stone, concrete, etc., built to protect a SCARP, EMBANKMENT or shore structure against erosion by waves of currents.

title: Glossary
heading: REYNOLDS NUMBER
text: The dimensionless ratio of the inertial force to the viscous force in fluid motion, where L is a characteristic length, γ the kinematic viscosity, and V a characteristic velocity. The Reynolds number is of importance in the theory of hydrodynamic stability and the origin of turbulence.

title: Glossary
heading: RIA
text: A long, narrow inlet, with depth gradually diminishing inward. Shorter and shallower than a FJORD.

title: Glossary
heading: RIDGE AND RUNNEL
text: Beach topography consisting of sand bars that have welded to the shore during the recovery stage after a storm. At low tide, water ponds in the runnels and flows seaward through gaps in the ridge (see Figure IV-2-31).

title: Glossary
heading: RIDGE, BEACH
text: A nearly continuous mound of beach material that has been shaped by wave or other action. Ridges may occur singly or as a series of approximately parallel deposits.

title: Glossary
heading: RILL MARKS
text: Tiny drainage channels in a beach caused by the flow seaward of water left in the sands of the upper part of the beach after the retreat of the tide or after the dying down of storm waves.

title: Glossary
heading: RIP
text: A body of water made rough by waves meeting an opposing current, particularly a tidal current; often found where tidal currents are converging and sinking.

title: Glossary
heading: RIP CHANNEL
text: A channel cut by seaward flow of RIP CURRENT, usually crosses a LONGSHORE BAR.

title: Glossary
heading: RIP CURRENT
text: A strong surface current flowing seaward from the shore. It usually appears as a visible band of agitated water and is the return movement of water piled up on the shore by incoming waves and wind. With the seaward movement concentrated in a limited band its velocity is somewhat accentuated. A rip consists of three parts: the FEEDER CURRENTS flowing parallel to the shore inside the breakers; the NECK, where the feeder currents converge and flow through the breakers in a narrow band or "rip"; and the HEAD OF RIP, where the current widens and slackens outside the breaker line. A rip current is often miscalled a rip tide. Also called RIP SURF.

title: Glossary
heading: RIP SURF
text: See RIP CURRENT.

title: Glossary
heading: RIP TIDE
text: Incorrect term for RIP CURRENT.

title: Glossary
heading: RIPARIAN
text: (1) Pertaining to the banks of a body of water. (2) Of, on or pertaining to the banks of a river.

title: Glossary
heading: RIPPLE
text: (1) The ruffling of the surface of water; hence, a little curling wave or undulation. (2) A wave less than 0.05 meter (2 inches) long controlled to a significant degree by both surface tension and gravity. See CAPILLARY WAVE and GRAVITY WAVE.

title: Glossary
heading: RIPPLE MARKS
text: Undulations produced by fluid movement over sediments. Oscillatory currents produce symmetric ripples whereas a well-defined current direction produces asymmetrical ripples. The crest line of ripples may be straight or sinuous. The characteristic features of ripples depend upon current velocity, particle size, persistence of current direction and whether the fluid is air or water. Sand DUNES may be regarded as a special kind of 'super'-ripple.

title: Glossary
heading: RIPPLES (bed forms)
text: Small bed forms with wavelengths less than 0.3 m (1 foot) and heights less than 0.03 m (0.1 foot).

title: Glossary
heading: RIPRAP
text: A protective layer or facing of quarrystone, usually well graded within wide size limit, randomly placed to prevent erosion, scour, or sloughing of an embankment or bluff; also the stone so used. The quarrystone is placed in a layer at least twice the thickness of the 50 percent size, or 1.25 times the thickness of the largest size stone in the gradation.

title: Glossary
heading: RISK ANALYSIS
text: Assessment of the total risk due to all possible environmental inputs and all possible mechanisms.

title: Glossary
heading: ROADSTEAD
text: An area of the sea used for anchorage of vessels and transhipment of cargo, usually without the protection from weather associated with harbors.

title: Glossary
heading: ROCK WEATHERING
text: Physical and mineralogical decay processes in rock brought about by exposure to climatic conditions either at the present time or in the geological past.

title: Glossary
heading: ROCK
text: (1) An aggregate of one or more minerals; or a body of undifferentiated mineral matter (e.g., obsidian). The three classes of rocks are: (a) Igneous – crystalline rocks formed from molten material. Examples are granite and basalt. (b) Sedimentary – resulting from the consolidation of loose sediment that has accumulated in layers. Examples are sandstone, shale and limestone. (c) Metamorphic – formed from preexisting rock as a result of burial, heat, and pressure. (2) A rocky mass lying at or near the surface of the water or along a jagged coastline, especially where dangerous to shipping.

title: Glossary
heading: ROLLER
text: An indefinite term, sometimes considered to denote one of a series of long-crested, large waves which roll in on a shore, as after a storm.

title: Glossary
heading: ROTARY CURRENT, TIDAL
text: A tidal current that flows continually with the direction of flow changing through all points of the compass during the tidal period. Rotary currents are usually found offshore where the direction of flow is not restricted by any barriers. The tendency for the rotation in direction has its origin in the deflecting force of the earth's rotation and, unless modified by local conditions, the change is clockwise in the Northern Hemisphere and counterclockwise in the Southern Hemisphere. The velocity of the current usually varies throughout the tidal cycle, passing through two maxima in approximately opposite directions and two minima with the direction of the current at approximately ninety degrees from the direction at the time of maximum velocity.

title: Glossary
heading: RUBBLE
text: (1) Loose angular waterworn stones along a beach. (2) Rough, irregular fragments of broken rock.

title: Glossary
heading: RUBBLE-MOUND STRUCTURE
text: A mound of random-shaped and random-placed stones protected with a cover layer of selected stones or specially shaped concrete armor units. (Armor units in a primary cover layer may be placed in an orderly manner or dumped at random.)

title: Glossary
heading: RUNUP, RUNDOWN
text: The upper and lower levels reached by a wave on a beach or coastal structure, relative to still-water level.

title: Glossary
heading: RUNNEL
text: A corrugation or trough formed in the FORESHORE or in the bottom just offshore by waves or tidal currents (see RIDGE and RUNNEL). S

title: Glossary
heading: S-SLOPE BREAKWATER
text: Rubble mound with gentle slope around still-water level and steeper slopes above and below.

title: Glossary
heading: SALIENT
text: A bulge in the coastline projecting towards an offshore island or breakwater, but not connected to it as in the case of a TOMBOLO - see also Ness and Cusp. Developed by WAVE REFRACTION and diffraction and long shore drift.

title: Glossary
heading: SALINITY
text: Number of grams of salt per thousand grams of sea water, usually expressed in parts per thousand (symbol: ‰).

title: Glossary
heading: SALINITY GRADIENT
text: Change in salinity with expressed in parts per thousand per foot.

title: Glossary
heading: SALT MARSH
text: A marsh periodically flooded by salt water (also tidal marsh ; sea marsh ).

title: Glossary
heading: SALT-WEDGE ESTUARY
text: In this circulation type, the density-driven component dominates and two well-mixed layers are separated by a sharp HALOCLINE. The seawater entering the channel appears as a tongue or wedge.

title: Glossary
heading: SALTATION
text: That method of sand movement in a fluid in which individual particles leave the bed by bounding nearly vertically and, because the motion of the fluid is not strong or turbulent enough to retain them in suspension, return to the bed at some distance downstream. The travel path of the particles is a series of hops and bounds.

title: Glossary
heading: SAND
text: Sediment particles, often largely composed of quartz, with a diameter of between 0.062 mm and 2 mm, generally classified as fine, medium, coarse or very coarse. Beach sand may sometimes be composed of organic sediments such as calcareous reef debris or shell fragments.

title: Glossary
heading: SAND BAR
text: (1) See BAR. (2) In a river, a ridge of sand built to or near the surface by river currents.

title: Glossary
heading: SAND BYPASSING
text: See BYPASSING, SAND.

title: Glossary
heading: SAND DUNE
text: A DUNE formed of sand.

title: Glossary
heading: SAND REEF
text: See BAR.

title: Glossary
heading: SAND SPIT
text: A narrow sand EMBANKMENT, created by an excess of deposition at its seaward terminus, with its distal end (the end away from the point of origin) terminating in open water.

title: Glossary
heading: SAND WAVES
text: (1) Longshore sand waves are large-scale features that maintain form while migrating along the shore with speeds on the order of kilometers per year. (2) Large-scale asymmetrical bedforms in sandy river beds having high length to height ratios and continuous crestlines.

title: Glossary
heading: SCARP, BEACH
text: An almost vertical slope along the beach caused by erosion by wave action. It may vary in height from a few cm to a meter or so, depending on wave action and the nature and composition of the beach. (See Figure A-1) See also ESCARPMENT.

title: Glossary
heading: SCATTER DIAGRAM
text: A two-dimensional histogram showing the joint probability density of two variables within a data sample.

title: Glossary
heading: SCOUR
text: Removal of underwater material by waves and currents, especially at the base or toe of a shore structure.

title: Glossary
heading: SCOUR PROTECTION
text: Protection against erosion of the seabed in front of the toe.

title: Glossary
heading: SEA
text: (1) A large body of salt water, second in rank to an ocean, more or less landlocked and generally part of, or connected with, an ocean or a larger sea. Examples: Mediterranean Sea; South China Sea. (2) Waves caused by wind at the place and time of observation. (3) State of the ocean or lake surface, in regard to waves.

title: Glossary
heading: SEA BREEZE
text: A light wind blowing from the sea toward the land caused by unequal heating of land and water masses.

title: Glossary
heading: SEA CHANGE
